Horseback riding through the surrounding forest and mountain terrain is available and offers a different pace and perspective than walking — the forest paths and mountain approaches take on a different quality from the saddle, and the tradition of mountain horses in this part of the Caucasus gives the activity an authenticity that more resort-style riding elsewhere lacks.

The Lechkhumi context

The Kulbaki Lakes make more sense understood within their region. Lechkhumi is a small highland area with its own deeply rooted wine tradition, its own architecture, and a way of life that has changed more slowly than in the country's more developed parts. Its wines are a genuine, if little-known, highlight: the Tvishi appellation — a Protected Designation of Origin producing a naturally semi-sweet white from the Tsolikouri grape, grown on the steep Rioni-valley terraces around the villages of Tvishi and Alpana — is among the most refined of Georgia's semi-sweet whites. Lechkhumi is also home to the rare and highly prized Usakhelouri (from the Zubi-Okureshi area, one of the most valuable grapes in the region) and the red Orbeluri Ojaleshi — a wine culture that punches well above the region's size. The regional center of Tsageri, the nearest town to the lakes, provides the practical services and accommodation base, and the wider Lechkhumi valley drive — through small villages, past traditional houses, along the rivers draining westward toward Imereti — gives the lakes the regional context that makes them feel part of a specific place rather than just a scenic stop.

Getting there and when to go

  • Location: Forested highlands near Tsageri, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia; ~955–990 m elevation.
  • Getting there: By car from Tsageri, the approach road climbing through forest to the lakes; conditions vary with season and weather, and while a standard vehicle manages in dry summer conditions, a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable for the final stages, especially after rain. From Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Tsageri, allow about 4.5–5 hours.
  • Time needed: A relaxed half-day at the lakes; longer (with an overnight) if combining with the Askhi/Lebarde hiking above.
  • When to go: May–October, with June–September the most reliable for accessible roads, full lake levels, and the cool forest climate that makes Kulbaki a fine summer retreat from the lowland heat. October brings autumn color to the deciduous forest — visually among the best times — though days shorten and roads grow less reliable toward winter.
  • Combine with: A wider Lechkhumi and western-Georgia trip — Racha to the east, Svaneti to the north via the Lechkhumi–Svaneti road, the Imereti sites around Kutaisi to the southwest, and the rest of the Lechkhumi cluster (the Ghvirishi Waterfall, Khvamli Mountain, the Tvishi wine villages, Dekhviri).

faq.title: What are the Kulbaki Lakes?
faq.content: The Kulbaki Lakes are two small forest lakes in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia, near the town of Tsageri at about 955–990 meters elevation. The larger Green Lake (Mtsvane Tba) has a calm, reflective surface and a small trout farm; the smaller Babushkino Lake is shallower and more seasonal. Set in a wooded mountain hollow, they're a quiet, accessible nature destination with a cool forest climate, and they also serve as a base for hiking up into the Askhi plateau and the alpine Lebarde lakes above. They're one of the more rewarding stops in the little-visited Lechkhumi region.

faq.title: What's the difference between the two lakes?
faq.content: The Green Lake is the larger, more developed, and more photogenic — created, by local accounts, from former marshland by a resident who built a dam and a trout farm, so it has a stable water level, a fish population, and some basic facilities (and a cottage). Its still, forest-reflecting surface makes it the more visually striking of the two. Babushkino Lake is smaller, shallower, and seasonal — it can partly dry out in hot, dry summers — and feels wilder and less managed. Seeing both gives a fuller experience: the calm, accessible Green Lake and the more naturalistic Babushkino.

faq.title: Can you hike from the Kulbaki Lakes?
faq.content: Yes — this is a big part of their appeal. The lakes sit at the foot of more serious mountain terrain, and trails from Kulbaki lead up into the Askhi massif (a remote limestone plateau) and toward the alpine Lebarde lakes above the treeline, which offer the high-mountain panoramas the forested lakes themselves don't. These are demanding routes for fit hikers (often with an overnight), quite different from the easy lakeside visit, so they're best done with proper preparation or a guide. Horseback riding through the surrounding forest is also available for a different pace.

faq.title: What is Lechkhumi known for besides the lakes?
faq.content: Wine, above all. Despite its small size, Lechkhumi is one of Georgia's notable wine regions: the Tvishi appellation (a Protected Designation of Origin) makes a refined, naturally semi-sweet white from the Tsolikouri grape on the steep Rioni-valley terraces around Tvishi and Alpana, and the region is also home to the rare, highly prized Usakhelouri and the red Orbeluri Ojaleshi. Beyond wine, Lechkhumi offers the dramatic Ghvirishi Waterfall, the mythic Khvamli Mountain, the Dekhviri fortress, and a slow, traditional way of life — making the Kulbaki Lakes one stop in a rewarding off-the-beaten-path region.

faq.title: How do you get to the Kulbaki Lakes, and when should you go?
faq.content: The lakes are reached by car from Tsageri, the Lechkhumi regional center; from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Tsageri, allow about 4.5–5 hours. The approach road climbs through forest, and while a standard car manages in dry summer conditions, a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable for the final stretch, especially after rain. The season is May–October — June to September for the most reliable roads, full lake levels, and the cool forest climate that makes it a pleasant summer retreat, and October for autumn forest color (though days shorten and roads worsen toward winter).

choleviLake.heroTitle: Cholevi Lake (Lake of Love): A Mountain Lake in Racha
choleviLake.content:
Cholevi Lake — also known as Lake Mravaldzali, and popularly as the “Lake of Love” — is a mountain lake in the Oni municipality of Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia (the country in the South Caucasus). It sits at around 1,840 meters above sea level near the village of Mravaldzali, ringed by dense forest and mountain peaks, its greenish water set in one of the quieter corners of Upper Racha. The lake is best known for its distinctive shape, which has earned it the “Lake of Love” nickname and made it a favorite of photographers, and for the calm, scenic setting that rewards a relaxed visit — picnicking, fishing, a boat on the water, or simply the walk around the shore. It’s one of the more rewarding natural stops in a region that still sees far fewer visitors than Kakheti, Svaneti, or Georgia’s headline mountain destinations.

What is Cholevi Lake?

Cholevi Lake (also called Lake Mravaldzali, or the “Lake of Love”) is a forest-ringed mountain lake at about 1,840 meters elevation near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Racha, western Georgia. Surrounded by dense forest and mountain peaks, with greenish water and a distinctive shape that gives it its romantic nickname, it’s a calm, scenic spot popular for picnics, fishing, and boat rides, with the finest panorama looking down from nearby Mount Khikhati. Less famous than Racha’s headline sights, it’s a peaceful, accessible lake set in the quiet beauty of Upper Racha — especially striking in autumn.

The setting

Cholevi Lake sits in the forested mountain terrain of Upper Racha, where the southern slopes of the Greater Caucasus carry dense mixed forest up into the highlands. At around 1,840 meters, it’s a genuine mountain lake — but a forested one, ringed by trees and peaks rather than the bare rock and snow of the highest alpine basins, which gives it an enclosed, sheltered character. The water carries greenish shades, and on still days its calm surface reflects the surrounding forest and sky in a composition that shifts through the day with the light and the wind. The most expansive view of the lake opens from above, on Mount Khikhati, which looks down over the water and out across the surrounding Rachan mountains.

The seasons change the lake’s character considerably. Spring brings the rapid greening of the deciduous forest and the wildflowers — the lake’s surroundings are known for primroses, among the first flowers to bloom after winter. Summer is the fullest green, the canopy at its densest and the water reflecting deep greens. Autumn is the most striking season, when the mixed forest turns through gold, orange, and red and the lake mirrors the colored canopy — the time most photographers aim for. Winter brings a quiet isolation, the bare deciduous trees opening views that the summer canopy hides and the snow giving the setting a stark, stripped-down character.

What to do at the lake

Despite its out-of-the-way location, Cholevi Lake is a place set up for relaxed enjoyment rather than a wilderness you simply look at. The shore has picnic areas, the lake offers good fishing, and there’s the option of a boat ride on the water — so a visit can be a leisurely few hours rather than a quick photo stop. Walking around the shoreline, settling in for a picnic, watching the light change on the water, or climbing toward Mount Khikhati for the panorama are the natural ways to spend time here. Its appeal is the combination of a scenic, distinctively shaped mountain lake with a calm, unhurried atmosphere — the quiet that Racha still offers and the busier parts of Georgia increasingly don’t.

Mravaldzali village

The nearby village of Mravaldzali is part of the experience. It has the handsome stone houses characteristic of Racha and wide views of the Caucasus, and it’s home to an 11th-century hall-type Church of St. George, which preserves inscriptions and fragments of art from the 11th century — a reminder that this quiet corner of Racha has deep historical roots alongside its natural appeal. The lake and the village together make a rewarding pairing of nature and heritage.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Mravaldzali village, Oni municipality, Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia; ~1,840 m elevation.
  • Getting there: Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Ambrolauri — roughly a half-day drive (around 4–5 hours to the region). A car is the practical way to reach the lake area, given limited public transport, and local guesthouses are the best source of current road and access information.
  • What’s there: Picnic areas, fishing, and boat rides; no major tourism infrastructure beyond this.
  • Best time: Late spring through autumn — summer for green and warmth (the lake is best for boating/fishing rather than swimming), and autumn for the forest color. Winter access is unreliable.
  • Combine with: Other Racha sights — the Shaori Reservoir (the lake lies on the Shkmeri–Shaori hiking route over Mount Khikhati), Nikortsminda cathedral, Ambrolauri and the Khvanchkara wine villages, and Upper Racha’s mountain country around Oni and Shovi.

faq.title: What is Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: Cholevi Lake — also known as Lake Mravaldzali, or popularly the “Lake of Love” — is a forest-ringed mountain lake at about 1,840 meters elevation near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Racha, western Georgia. Surrounded by dense forest and mountain peaks, with greenish water and a distinctive shape that earned its romantic nickname, it’s a calm, scenic spot popular with photographers and visitors, with picnic areas, fishing, and the option of a boat ride. Less famous than Racha’s headline sights, it’s one of the more rewarding quiet stops in this overlooked highland region.

faq.title: Why is it called the “Lake of Love”?
faq.content: The “Lake of Love” (Sikvaruli) nickname comes from the lake’s distinctive shape, which has made it a popular spot for photographers and couples. Its other names are Cholevi Lake and Lake Mravaldzali, after the nearby village of Mravaldzali. The combination of the romantic name, the scenic forest-and-mountain setting, and the calm water has given it a quiet fame within Racha, though it remains far less visited than Georgia’s better-known lakes.

faq.title: What can you do at Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: It’s set up for relaxed enjoyment rather than just looking. The shore has picnic areas, the lake offers good fishing, and you can take a boat out on the water, so a visit can easily be a leisurely few hours. You can walk around the shoreline, picnic with the forest-and-mountain views, or climb toward nearby Mount Khikhati for the finest panorama down over the lake and across the Rachan mountains. It’s most beautiful in autumn, when the surrounding forest turns gold and orange and the colors reflect in the water.

faq.title: Where is Cholevi Lake, and how do you get there?
faq.content: It’s near the village of Mravaldzali in the Oni municipality of Upper Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia, at about 1,840 meters. Racha is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi and Ambrolauri — roughly a half-day drive. A car is the practical way to reach the lake area, as public transport is limited, and local guesthouses are the best source of current road and access details. It lies on the hiking route between Shkmeri village and the Shaori Reservoir, over Mount Khikhati.

faq.title: What else is near Cholevi Lake?
faq.content: The nearby village of Mravaldzali has handsome traditional Rachan stone houses, Caucasus views, and an 11th-century hall-type Church of St. George with original inscriptions and art fragments. Further afield, the lake pairs well with Racha’s other attractions: the large Shaori Reservoir, the richly carved Nikortsminda cathedral, the town of Ambrolauri and the famous Khvanchkara wine villages, and the high mountain country of Upper Racha around Oni and Shovi. Together they make Racha a rewarding region for travelers who prefer quiet highland landscapes and wine to the busy main circuit.

khvanchkaraMonument.heroTitle: The Khvanchkara Wine Monument: Georgia's Giant Wine Bottle
khvanchkaraMonument.content:
The Khvanchkara Wine Monument is a landmark in Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha in northwestern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — in the heart of the region that produces one of the country’s most celebrated wines. It takes the form of a giant wine bottle, a large and unmissable sculpture standing in the town that serves as the gateway to the Khvanchkara wine zone — the small stretch of the Rioni valley where the indigenous Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes ripen into the naturally semi-sweet red wine that carries the village’s name. More than a simple marker, the monument is a deliberate civic tribute: a statement of how completely this quiet mountain region identifies with its famous wine. For travelers passing through Ambrolauri on a Racha trip, it’s a quick, free, and characterful stop that anchors the abstract fame of Khvanchkara to a real place in the landscape.

What is the Khvanchkara Wine Monument?

The Khvanchkara Wine Monument is a large sculpture in the shape of a wine bottle, standing in a square in the town of Ambrolauri, the center of the Racha region in western Georgia. It honors Khvanchkara — Georgia’s celebrated naturally semi-sweet red wine, made from the Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes grown in a small microzone around the nearby village of Khvanchkara. A prominent, photogenic town landmark rather than a remote marker, it’s an easy free stop on a Racha itinerary and a fitting symbol of a region whose identity is bound up with one of Georgia’s most famous wines.

Khvanchkara wine

The wine the monument honors is among the most distinctively Georgian of all Georgian wines — not the most exported, but among the most deeply tied to a single place and a particular set of cultural associations. Khvanchkara is a naturally semi-sweet red made from two indigenous grapes, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli, grown in a microzone of very limited extent on the right bank of the Rioni valley in Racha (on the slopes of the Likhi ridge, roughly 450–800 meters up). The area that can legally carry the Khvanchkara designation is small — around 900 hectares — so total production is modest by any commercial standard, which has long given the wine a prestige and scarcity within Georgia beyond what quality alone would explain. It is one of Georgia’s oldest Protected Designations of Origin, recognized as a PDO since 1932.

The semi-sweetness is natural, not the result of arrested fermentation or added sugar: the specific microclimate lets the grapes accumulate enough sugar that fermentation stops naturally before all of it converts to alcohol, leaving residual sweetness. That site-dependent process is exactly why the designation is geographically restricted — the same grapes grown elsewhere in Racha or beyond don’t reliably produce the same result. Indeed, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli are so particular to this corner of Racha-Lechkhumi that attempts to grow them elsewhere change their character entirely, which is part of why Khvanchkara cannot be reproduced anywhere else.

A wine with a story

Khvanchkara’s history is richer than its fame as a sweet red suggests. The blend was created in the late 19th century by the local noblemen Luarsab and Dimitri Kipiani, and was originally known as “Sakipiano” wine (the Kipianis’ wine). In 1907 it won the Grand Prix at an international exposition in Belgium, sent there by the wine merchants Nestor Tsereteli and Varlam Papuashvili — an early mark of international recognition. When the Soviet regime industrialized wine production, it renamed the wine “Khvanchkara” after the village where the best Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grew, in part to erase the Kipiani name from public memory. The wine is also widely said to have been a favorite of Joseph Stalin — himself a Georgian — an association often repeated as part of its Soviet-era celebrity, though, like much Stalin lore, it’s better treated as part of the wine’s legend than as documented fact.

Made by serious producers from genuine-microzone grapes, Khvanchkara is a wine of real character — its berry-rich, raspberry-toned sweetness balanced by the natural acidity of mountain-grown fruit and the structure of the Aleksandrouli grape, giving it a complexity that simple sweetness alone could never produce.

The monument and its setting

The monument stands in Ambrolauri, the small regional capital of Racha, set among the forested hills and the vineyard country of the lower Rioni valley. Ambrolauri is a quiet town, undisturbed by the kind of wine-tourism infrastructure that fills Georgia’s more developed wine regions, and the giant-bottle monument gives the town an immediate, slightly playful identity — an unambiguous statement of what this place is known for. A stop here is brief — five to ten minutes for photographs and a moment to register the geography of one of Georgia’s most famous wine designations — and its value is as a symbol that anchors the idea of Khvanchkara to a real place, giving the wine a geographical reality that tasting it elsewhere cannot.

Combining with the wider Racha itinerary

The monument fits naturally into a Racha trip built around Ambrolauri as a base. From here, the family wine producers of the Khvanchkara microzone are within easy reach — the small cellars in the surrounding villages (Khvanchkara, Akhalsopeli, and others) where the wine is made in qvevri by traditional methods and where tastings are often wonderfully informal: a knock at the gate frequently earns a glass filled to the brim and a plate of local food, more than any formal appointment. The wider Racha circuit pairs the monument and the cellars with the richly carved Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, the Cholevi (Lake of Love) and other Racha lakes, and the high mountain country of Upper Racha around Oni and Shovi. For anyone interested in Georgian wine, this short, free roadside stop carries a symbolic weight out of all proportion to the few minutes it takes.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: In a town square in Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha, in the Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region of western Georgia.
  • Getting there: Ambrolauri is reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi — roughly a half-day drive (the E60 to Kutaisi, then up the Rioni valley). Daily marshrutkas run from Kutaisi to Ambrolauri; a hired car gives more freedom (and lets you stop at the Nakerala Pass viewpoint en route). The monument is in the town itself, impossible to miss.
  • Time needed: 5–10 minutes; longer if you base in Ambrolauri to explore the wine villages.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Combine with: The Khvanchkara wine cellars, Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, Cholevi Lake, and Upper Racha.

faq.title: What is the Khvanchkara Wine Monument?
faq.content: It’s a large sculpture in the shape of a wine bottle, standing in a square in the town of Ambrolauri, the center of the Racha region in western Georgia. It honors Khvanchkara — Georgia’s celebrated naturally semi-sweet red wine, made from the Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grapes grown in a small microzone around the nearby village of Khvanchkara. A prominent, photogenic town landmark, it’s an easy free stop on a Racha trip and a fitting symbol of a region whose identity is bound up with one of Georgia’s most famous wines.

faq.title: What is Khvanchkara wine?
faq.content: Khvanchkara is a naturally semi-sweet red Georgian wine made from two indigenous grapes, Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli, grown in a small microzone on the right bank of the Rioni valley in Racha. Its sweetness is natural — the local microclimate lets the grapes ripen enough that fermentation stops on its own, leaving residual sugar — which is why the same grapes grown elsewhere can’t reproduce it. One of Georgia’s oldest Protected Designations of Origin (a PDO since 1932), it’s prized for its berry-rich, raspberry-toned flavor balanced by mountain-grown acidity, and it’s made in only modest quantities, which adds to its prestige within Georgia.

faq.title: Why is Khvanchkara wine so famous?
faq.content: Several reasons. It’s a naturally semi-sweet red that genuinely can’t be reproduced outside its tiny Racha microzone, which gives it real rarity. It has a notable history: the blend was created in the late 19th century by the noblemen Luarsab and Dimitri Kipiani (originally called “Sakipiano” wine) and won the Grand Prix at a 1907 exposition in Belgium. The Soviet regime renamed it after the village of Khvanchkara, and it’s also widely said to have been a favorite of Stalin — a piece of lore that, true or not, added to its fame across the USSR. Today it’s a point of national pride in Georgian winemaking.

faq.title: Are Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grown anywhere else?
faq.content: Essentially no — and that’s central to Khvanchkara’s identity. Both grapes are particular to the Racha-Lechkhumi area, and Aleksandrouli in particular changes character dramatically when grown elsewhere, so attempts to cultivate it in other regions don’t produce comparable wine. By legal definition, true Khvanchkara can only be made from Aleksandrouli and Mujuretuli grown in the Khvanchkara microzone. This is why the wine, unlike many semi-sweet wines, genuinely cannot be replicated anywhere else in the world.

faq.title: How do you visit the monument, and what else is nearby?
faq.content: The monument is in the town of Ambrolauri, the regional center of Racha, reached from Tbilisi via Kutaisi (about a half-day drive) — daily marshrutkas run from Kutaisi, or a hired car gives more freedom. It’s right in the town and impossible to miss, a quick free photo stop. The real reward is using Ambrolauri as a base to visit the family wine cellars of the Khvanchkara villages, where tastings are charmingly informal, alongside the carved Nikortsminda Cathedral, the Shaori Reservoir, Cholevi Lake, and the mountain country of Upper Racha.

khvamliMountain.heroTitle: Khvamli Mountain: Myth, Caves, and Views in Lechkhumi
khvamliMountain.content:
Khvamli (also known as Khomli) is a limestone massif rising to about 2,002 meters in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — on the border of the Tsageri and Tskaltubo municipalities. Its cliffs and forested slopes are visible from the valley towns below, and its summit gives panoramic views that reach, on clear days, across the breadth of the western Caucasus: the Lechkhumi valley below, the Racha highlands to the east, the Imereti lowlands and Kutaisi to the south, and the Greater Caucasus along the northern horizon. It is geologically distinctive — a karst limestone formation standing apart from the surrounding terrain, presenting a sheer southern bluff some 300 meters high — and one of the most mythologically charged mountains in Georgia, woven through with legends of chained titans, hidden royal treasure, and ancient ritual.

What is Khvamli Mountain?

Khvamli is a 2,002-meter limestone massif in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia, on the Tsageri–Tskaltubo municipal border, about 55 km from Kutaisi. It's known for three things: its sweeping summit panoramas over western Georgia; its caves — including Tekenteri, entered through the hollow of a giant beech tree, and the Boga ice cave — some bearing medieval cave paintings; and its deep mythology, linking it to the Prometheus/Amirani fire-bringer legend and to a famous tradition that the treasures of the Georgian kings were hidden in its caves. A St. George church near the summit hosts the annual Khvamloba pilgrimage. Reached by car plus a short hike, it's one of the most atmospheric destinations in the little-visited Lechkhumi region.

The mythology

Khvamli is bound up with the legend of Amirani, the Georgian (and broader Caucasian) hero of the fire-bringer myth — the figure widely regarded as the Caucasian counterpart to the Greek Prometheus. In the story, a hero who gives fire or defies the gods is punished by being chained to a mountain in the Caucasus, a bird sent to torment him; Khvamli is one of the peaks traditionally identified with that imprisonment. The relationship between the Georgian Amirani tradition and the Greek Prometheus myth has long been discussed — the two are close parallels, and some see Amirani as an older or independent Caucasian root of the same ancient story — and Khvamli sits at the heart of this lore. The mountain also appears in the wider Greek mythological geography of the region, named among the places the Argonauts and Heracles were said to have traveled in the land of Colchis. Standing on the summit, over a landscape where these stories have been told for millennia, gives the mountain a presence that purely scenic places, however striking, rarely match.

The legends of treasure

Alongside the Prometheus association runs Khvamli's most famous Georgian legend: that the treasures of the Georgian kings were hidden in its caves, concealed during invasions — above all the Mongol invasions of the 13th century — to keep them from capture. The tradition is old and documented: Khvamli appears in the medieval Georgian Chronicles, and one of its caves carries the name Kvab-Sakhizari — roughly, the cave "impregnable to enemies, full of the treasures of kings." The story has drawn treasure-seekers for centuries. In one well-known episode from the Soviet era, Stalin is said to have sent the famous Georgian mountaineer Aliosha Japaridze to climb Khvamli in search of the legendary hoard — he reportedly came back empty-handed. Other layers of folklore have gathered here too: sacred springs, weather-divining traditions (the mountain as the seat of a thunder-and-weather power, watched by locals to read the coming weather), and the sense of a place to be approached with respect — the natural accumulation of meaning around an isolated, imposing, geologically unusual massif.

The caves

The caves running through Khvamli's limestone are the physical anchor for these stories. Tekenteri is the most singular: a karst shaft about 16 meters deep, entered through the hollow of a venerable beech tree, connecting to a horizontal tunnel with a spring at the bottom — old baskets found inside attest to its long human use. The Boga ice cave holds year-round sub-freezing conditions in its interior, where the air-circulation dynamics of the cave preserve ice formations alongside the rock. Several of the caves contain medieval paintings in red pigment, dating to the High Middle Ages, evidence of religious or ritual use beyond simple shelter — one more reason Khvamli has long felt, to the communities around it, like a place where ordinary rules don't quite apply.

A note of caution: the caves are wild monuments, not developed show-caves (unlike the nearby Prometheus Cave near Tskaltubo). Tekenteri's shaft and the karst terrain generally are genuinely hazardous without proper equipment and knowledge — the caves are best appreciated for their stories and settings rather than entered casually.

The hike and the summit

The approach climbs from the valley by a mountain road through the forested lower slopes, gaining height through mixed woodland toward the subalpine zone. Road conditions vary with season and weather, and a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable — especially after rain, when the unpaved sections turn muddy and loose. The road cuts the hiking distance substantially: most visitors drive to the upper road terminus, then walk one to three hours depending on the viewpoints and features they want to reach.

The St. George church near the summit is the principal built structure and a useful landmark on the upper terrain. It is the focus of Khvamloba, the annual festival held on the eighth Saturday after Orthodox Easter (in early summer), when people from the surrounding communities gather on the mountain for religious observance, communal celebration, and the upkeep of the traditions tied to the site. Attending Khvamloba offers a direct encounter with the living relationship between the Lechkhumi communities and their mountain that an ordinary hiking visit can't provide.

The views

The panoramas from Khvamli's upper sections are among the most expansive accessible anywhere in the western Caucasus. The summit elevation, combined with the open karst ridges that give unobstructed sightlines in every direction, lends the views a completeness that enclosed or forested viewpoints can't match. On especially clear days the city of Kutaisi is visible in the Rioni lowlands to the south — the distance from a mountain summit to a regional capital laid out plainly across the land, a direct read on the geography linking the Lechkhumi highlands to western Georgia's urban center below.

The light at sunset is especially rewarding — the limestone faces catching the low western sun and warming against the darkening forest below — and the combination of the mythology, the commanding position, and the evening light makes a sunset visit one of the more memorable moments of a western Georgia trip for many who experience it.

Practical considerations and safety

The mountain weather changes fast and can be severe: the exposed limestone terrain catches the weather systems moving through the western Caucasus, and clear conditions can deteriorate quickly. Carry warm and waterproof layers regardless of the starting weather, and be ready to descend if visibility drops or rain sets in. The karst terrain of the upper mountain includes sinkholes and cave openings that demand attention — stay on established paths and don't explore cave entrances without proper equipment and knowledge.

Practical information

  • Location: Lechkhumi highlands, on the Tsageri–Tskaltubo municipal border, Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region, western Georgia. Accessible from Tsageri, or from Kutaisi/Tskaltubo (~55 km / ~75 min to the trailhead area, then ~30–40 min ascent by road).
  • Altitude: ~2,002 m at the summit.
  • Getting there: By car to the upper road terminus, then on foot to the summit area and viewpoints. The mountain road needs a vehicle with good ground clearance, especially when wet; public transport is limited, so a car or hired driver/taxi is the practical option. Check current road conditions before setting out.
  • Duration: Half-day to full day depending on route and time at the summit.
  • Best season: May–October — summer for the clearest conditions, autumn for the most atmospheric light; the Khvamloba festival falls on the eighth Saturday after Orthodox Easter.
  • Admission: Free.

faq.title: What is Khvamli Mountain?
faq.content: Khvamli (also called Khomli) is a 2,002-meter limestone massif in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia, on the border of the Tsageri and Tskaltubo municipalities, about 55 km from Kutaisi. It's known for its sweeping summit views over western Georgia, its caves (including Tekenteri, entered through a hollow beech tree, and the Boga ice cave, some with medieval paintings), and its rich mythology — it's one of the mountains linked to the Prometheus/Amirani fire-bringer legend and to the famous tradition that the treasures of the Georgian kings were hidden in its caves. A St. George church near the summit hosts the annual Khvamloba pilgrimage.

faq.title: Why is Khvamli Mountain associated with Prometheus?
faq.content: Khvamli is one of the Caucasus peaks traditionally identified with the fire-bringer myth — the story of a hero chained to a mountain as punishment for giving fire to humanity. In Georgian tradition this hero is Amirani, widely regarded as the Caucasian counterpart to the Greek Prometheus; the two myths are close parallels, and the relationship between them has long been discussed, with some viewing Amirani as an older or independent Caucasian root of the same ancient story. Khvamli also appears in Greek mythological geography as a place linked to the Argonauts and Heracles in the land of Colchis, giving it a deep mythological resonance.

faq.title: Is it true that treasure is hidden in Khvamli's caves?
faq.content: It's one of Georgia's most enduring legends. Tradition — recorded as far back as the medieval Georgian Chronicles — holds that the treasures of the Georgian kings were hidden in Khvamli's caves during invasions, especially the Mongol invasions of the 13th century; one cave is even named Kvab-Sakhizari, the cave "impregnable to enemies, full of the treasures of kings." The story has drawn treasure-seekers for centuries. In one famous Soviet-era episode, Stalin is said to have sent the celebrated mountaineer Aliosha Japaridze to search the mountain for the hoard — he reportedly returned empty-handed. Whether any treasure was ever really there remains, fittingly, a mystery.

faq.title: Can you go inside Khvamli's caves?
faq.content: The caves are wild natural monuments, not developed show-caves like the nearby Prometheus Cave near Tskaltubo. Tekenteri is a roughly 16-meter karst shaft entered through the hollow of a beech tree, with a spring at the bottom; the Boga cave holds ice year-round. Some caves contain medieval red-pigment paintings. But the shafts and karst terrain are genuinely hazardous without proper caving equipment and experience, so the caves are best appreciated for their stories and settings rather than entered casually — and the upper mountain's sinkholes and cave openings mean you should stay on established paths.

faq.title: How do you get to Khvamli, and when should you go?
faq.content: Khvamli is reached most practically by car: from Kutaisi it's about 55 km (around 75 minutes) to the trailhead area via Tskaltubo and Tsageri, then a 30–40-minute drive up the mountain road to a parking area, from where it's a 1–3-hour walk to the summit viewpoints. The mountain road needs a vehicle with good ground clearance, especially when wet, and public transport is limited, so a car or hired driver is best. The season is May–October — summer for the clearest views, autumn for the best light; the Khvamloba festival falls on the eighth Saturday after Orthodox Easter. Bring warm, waterproof layers, as the weather changes fast.

ghvirishiWaterfall.heroTitle: Ghvirishi Waterfall: A Dramatic Cascade in Lechkhumi
ghvirishiWaterfall.content:
The Ghvirishi Waterfall is one of the most striking natural landmarks in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — near the village of Kveda Ghvirishi in the Tsageri municipality, at about 625 meters above sea level. It is among the more dramatic and photogenic waterfalls in this quiet corner of the country: a high fall that drops from a limestone cliff into a deep, narrow gorge, the surrounding steep forested slopes and pale rock faces giving the site a concentrated natural drama. A glass viewing platform built above the fall lets you look straight down onto the cascade and the ravine, and the spectacular cliff-edge drive in adds to the sense of occasion. For travelers exploring Lechkhumi — already among the least-visited parts of western Georgia — Ghvirishi is one of the region's strongest single natural attractions.

What is the Ghvirishi Waterfall?

The Ghvirishi Waterfall is a tall waterfall in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia, near Kveda Ghvirishi village in the Tsageri municipality, at about 625 m elevation. It plunges from a high limestone rock into a deep ravine, forming a small pool at the base. Its standout feature for visitors is a glass viewing platform built above the fall, giving a dramatic look down onto the cascade and the gorge — reached by a short walk (and a memorable cliff-edge approach road). It's at its most powerful in spring and early summer, when snowmelt and rain drive maximum flow. One of the highlights of a Lechkhumi trip, it combines naturally with the region's lakes, wine, and villages.

The waterfall and its setting

The fall descends from the edge of a limestone cliff into a gorge whose walls close in on both sides, into a rocky recess at the base — an enclosed space that amplifies both the sound and the visual impact of the water. The geology around it adds interest beyond the pure spectacle: the limestone formations, the water-carved recesses in the cliff faces, the small pools below. Limestone develops detailed, textured surfaces that respond to water in visually complex ways, and the rock around the Ghvirishi gorge carries the accumulated marks of water action over a long geological span, rewarding close looking as much as the obvious panorama.

The forested slopes descending toward the gorge add a biological richness to the geological drama — the dense, green western Georgian mixed forest above and around the fall, vegetation finding a hold in the limestone wherever water and soil collect, and the general lushness of the Lechkhumi landscape in the warmer months. The contrast between the deep green of the forest, the pale grey of the exposed limestone, and the white of the falling water is what makes Ghvirishi so rewarding to photograph in the right conditions.

The seasonal variation is significant and worth understanding before you go. Spring and early summer — roughly April through June, when snowmelt from the higher terrain combines with spring rain — bring the greatest water volume and the sharpest greenery; the fall is at its widest and most powerful, the spray reaching well out from the base, and the sound filling the gorge. Through high summer the flow eases as the snowmelt diminishes and the fall grows more slender — still effective, but quieter. Autumn brings the forest's color change and, if the rains are good, a renewed flow, giving the site a different but equally rewarding character.

The glass viewpoint

What sets Ghvirishi apart among western Georgia's waterfalls is the quality of its viewpoint. A glass viewing platform has been built above the fall, projecting out to give an elevated, exposed perspective looking down onto the cascade and into the gorge below — the full scene of waterfall, ravine, rock formations, and the broader Lechkhumi landscape beyond, without any technical descent into the canyon. It's among the more dramatic easily accessible viewpoints in this part of western Georgia, and the reason the site has built a real following among Georgian nature and landscape photographers.

The photography potential is high relative to the effort to reach it. The combination of waterfall, limestone gorge, forest, and panoramic view offers a variety of compositions within a compact area, and the mist that wet weather and the fall's own spray create in the gorge gives the site a particularly atmospheric quality in overcast conditions — something clear-weather shots can't replicate. Note that there may be a small admission charge for the viewing platform.

The approach and the drive

Reaching the waterfall involves a short walk from the road access point near Kveda Ghvirishi — straightforward and manageable for ordinary fitness on a dry day, over mostly easy-to-moderate terrain with some uneven sections that need attention underfoot. The walk to the main viewpoints is short enough to keep the visit accessible to a broad range of travelers rather than only hikers. The approach is part of the appeal: the road up climbs along dramatic cliff edges with spectacular views before reaching the waterfall point.

A genuine caution: the path and road become noticeably more demanding after rain — the limestone and forest paths turn slippery when wet — so checking recent weather and wearing footwear with good grip are sensible precautions for this specific site, not excessive ones. (For the adventurous, the Ghvirishi river is also known to canyoning enthusiasts.)

Local color: endemic wheat and village life

The Ghvirishi area carries a quiet cultural interest alongside the natural drama. Kveda Ghvirishi is known for ancient endemic wheat varieties — Zandura, Dika, Tsiteli Doli — disease-resistant heritage grains for which Georgia is one of the world's oldest sources, and the nearby Zanduri House, a family growing this unique wheat and making food from it, is a worthwhile stop for travelers interested in the region's living agricultural traditions. It's a small reminder that Lechkhumi's appeal is as much about its deep-rooted rural culture as its landscapes.

Visiting within Lechkhumi

Ghvirishi is most naturally visited as part of a broader Lechkhumi itinerary taking in the Kulbaki lakes, the Tvishi wine area (the village of Tvishi lies a little to the south), the character of the Tsageri district, and the connections northward toward Svaneti or eastward toward Racha. The waterfall itself takes 30–60 minutes for a thorough visit and pairs naturally with the forest lakes of Kulbaki in a single day that gives a good introduction to Lechkhumi's nature without a multi-day commitment. For those who do give the area several days, the combination of the Ghvirishi gorge, the Kulbaki lakes, and the hiking routes up toward the alpine terrain (the Askhi plateau) makes a natural itinerary with real variety and a progression from the accessible to the demanding.

Getting there and when to go

  • Location: Near Kveda Ghvirishi village, Tsageri municipality, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia; ~8–9 km south of Tsageri, at ~625 m.
  • Getting there: From Tsageri (the Lechkhumi regional center, ~5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi), via the Lechkhumi valley road (typically approached via Alpana). Roads are secondary highland roads — paved in parts, rougher in others, variable with weather; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is the practical choice. A short walk leads to the viewpoint.
  • Time needed: About 30–60 minutes at the waterfall.
  • Admission: Possibly a small fee for the viewing platform.
  • When to go: Spring to early summer (roughly April–June) for maximum flow and greenery; autumn for forest color; high summer for the most comfortable, reliable road and path conditions. Avoid right after heavy rain (slippery paths).

faq.title: What is the Ghvirishi Waterfall?
faq.content: The Ghvirishi Waterfall is a tall waterfall in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia, near the village of Kveda Ghvirishi in the Tsageri municipality, at about 625 meters above sea level. It plunges from a high limestone cliff into a deep, narrow gorge, forming a small pool at the base. Its standout feature is a glass viewing platform built above the fall, giving a dramatic view straight down onto the cascade and the ravine. It's one of the strongest single natural attractions in the quiet, little-visited Lechkhumi region.

faq.title: Is there really a glass viewing platform?
faq.content: Yes — a glass viewpoint has been built above the waterfall, where you can stand to look down onto the falling water and the gorge below and take in the wider Lechkhumi landscape. It gives the dramatic perspective of the gorge without any technical descent into the canyon, and it's a large part of why the site has become popular with Georgian landscape photographers. There may be a small admission charge to use it; it's worth confirming locally. Reaching it is a short walk from the road, and the cliff-edge approach drive is spectacular in its own right.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit the Ghvirishi Waterfall?
faq.content: For the most powerful, widest flow and the sharpest green surroundings, come in spring to early summer — roughly April through June — when snowmelt and spring rain feed the maximum water volume. Through high summer the flow eases and the fall becomes more slender (though summer offers the most reliable road and path conditions). Autumn brings the forest's color change and, after good rains, renewed flow. Whatever the season, avoid visiting right after heavy rain, as the limestone and forest paths become slippery.

faq.title: How do you get to the Ghvirishi Waterfall, and is it a hard walk?
faq.content: It's near Kveda Ghvirishi village in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, about 8–9 km south of Tsageri (the regional center, roughly 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi), usually approached via Alpana. The roads are secondary highland roads, paved in parts and rougher in others, so a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps. From the road access point it's a short walk to the viewpoint over mostly easy-to-moderate terrain with a few uneven sections — manageable for ordinary fitness on a dry day, but slippery and harder after rain, so wear shoes with good grip.

faq.title: What else is there to see around Ghvirishi?
faq.content: Quite a lot, if you take Lechkhumi slowly. The village of Kveda Ghvirishi is known for ancient endemic wheat varieties (Zandura, Dika, Tsiteli Doli), and the nearby Zanduri House offers a taste of the region's living agricultural traditions. The waterfall combines naturally with the Kulbaki lakes, the Tvishi wine area just to the south, the Dekhviri fortress, and — for hikers — the Askhi plateau above. Together these make Lechkhumi a rewarding, off-the-beaten-path region for travelers who prefer quiet landscapes and rural culture to the busy main circuit.

askhiMassif.heroTitle: The Askhi Massif: Georgia's Wild Limestone Plateau
askhiMassif.content:
The Askhi Massif is a vast limestone plateau and mountain complex in western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — straddling the highlands of the Lechkhumi and Samegrelo regions (on the borders of Svaneti to the north and Imereti to the south), rising to about 2,520 meters at Gadrekili Peak. It is one of the most geologically distinctive and least-explored mountain landscapes in the country: a large karst plateau whose combination of limestone geology, alpine terrain, extensive cave systems, and sweeping views over the western Georgian lowlands gives it a character unlike any other highland destination in Georgia. While the country's mountain tourism concentrates on the Greater Caucasus — Svaneti and Kazbegi — Askhi offers a wilderness experience of comparable physical scale and far greater solitude, its remoteness and difficult access having spared it the crowds that have transformed the famous ranges.

What is the Askhi Massif?

The Askhi Massif is a large limestone (karst) plateau in the western Georgian highlands, on the borders of the Lechkhumi and Samegrelo regions, rising to about 2,520 m at Gadrekili Peak. It's known for three things: dramatic karst scenery (cliffs, sinkholes, limestone pavement); more than 80 documented caves, including ice caves; and vast panoramic views over much of western Georgia. The classic way to experience it is a demanding two-day trek across the plateau between the villages of Zubi and Kulbaki, through alpine meadow and shepherd pasture with a wild camp on top. Remote, largely unmarked, and water-scarce on the plateau, it's a route for fit, experienced hikers — best done with a guide — between roughly June and October.

The geology

Askhi's defining feature is its limestone geology and the karst landscape that water has carved from it over millions of years. Limestone dissolves in slightly acidic water, and the combination of the high rainfall of the western Georgian Caucasus with the massif's thick limestone has produced one of the most extensively karstified mountain landscapes in the South Caucasus. The surface shows it everywhere — sinkholes where the ground has collapsed into voids below, limestone pavement grooved and furrowed by dissolution, dramatic cliff faces exposing the depth of the rock, and the cave entrances that punctuate the plateau as gateways to the system underground.

The cave systems are among the most significant in Georgia, giving Askhi a scientific and speleological importance beyond its scenery. The massif holds more than 80 documented karst caves, some of considerable length and depth, with stalactite and stalagmite formations, underground rivers and lakes, and — in the higher, deeper caves where temperatures stay below freezing year-round — permanent ice formations. Speleological exploration here has continued since the Soviet period, and the system is not yet fully mapped; new passages and chambers are still being documented.

The plateau landscape

Above ground, the plateau is a landscape of real visual power — open limestone terrain, alpine grassland on the higher sections, sheer cliff edges where the plateau drops to the forested valleys, and extensive views over several regions of western Georgia at once. Its plateau character — the sense of being on an elevated, expansive tableland rather than climbing toward a single summit — gives Askhi a spatial quality unlike the more typically Georgian Greater Caucasus, where the terrain is more vertical and the valleys more enclosed.

The summer pastures are used by shepherds who bring cattle and sheep up from the valleys for the grazing season, and the seasonal shepherd huts — small stone and timber structures dotting the plateau where the pasture is good — add a human presence that deepens rather than diminishes the sense of remoteness. The interplay of natural limestone terrain, managed summer grassland, and the wild alpine zones above produces a landscape of considerable variety across the plateau's extent.

The views from the plateau edges are among the most extensive accessible from any mountain terrain in western Georgia. On a clear day they take in the Lechkhumi valleys, the Samegrelo lowlands, the Imereti hills to the south, and the higher Greater Caucasus ranges to the north — even, in the clearest conditions, a distant glimpse of the Black Sea to the west. When cloud inversions form over the western lowlands, the plateau can stand clear above a sea of cloud filling the valleys below — the Gomismta-and-Bakhmaro experience, but at higher elevation and with a far more open landscape around it.

The main trekking route: Zubi to Kulbaki

The established crossing of the massif links the village of Zubi (east) with the Kulbaki area (west), traversing the plateau and giving access to the full range of its landscapes over a route of roughly 25–35 kilometers, depending on the line taken and detours to viewpoints and cave entrances. It's usually done as a two-day trek with a night camped on the plateau, though strong, experienced hikers with an early start and no major detours can do it in a single long day.

The structure is essentially a steep climbing day and a long descending day. The approach from Zubi climbs through forest before emerging onto the plateau — the most sustained physical effort of the route, with the climb alone gaining on the order of 1,600 meters. Once up, the plateau crossing moves through more varied terrain — open grassland, limestone pavement, cliff-edge viewpoints, and the ground around the more accessible cave entrances — at a pace that lets you engage with the landscape rather than just cover it. The descent toward Kulbaki drops back through forest to the Kulbaki lakes (the Green and Babushkino lakes, at much lower elevation), where there's a trout farm and a cottage — a welcome end point after the wild camp above. The total elevation gain over the full route can exceed 1,600 meters depending on the configuration.

The crossing demands a genuine baseline of fitness and proper mountain preparation: sturdy footwear for limestone that can be sharp and uneven; the capacity to carry enough water, since surface water is scarce on the plateau itself (what little exists should be boiled or filtered); camping equipment for the overnight, as there's no accommodation on the plateau; and weather-appropriate clothing for conditions that can swing from warm summer sun to cold cloud and rain in a single afternoon.

Navigation, conditions, and safety

The trail marking on Askhi is inconsistent and insufficient for confident independent navigation across the full crossing, and this is what most clearly separates it from Georgia's developed hiking destinations. The terrain isn't technically difficult in a mountaineering sense, but the karst topography — sinkholes, limestone pavement, and open grassland that gives no visual cue to the right line — is complex enough that crossing it without detailed route knowledge or GPS with downloaded tracks requires real experience and comfort with uncertain ground. A local guide with specific knowledge of the Askhi routes is strongly recommended for anyone unfamiliar with the plateau; guiding services in the Lechkhumi region and in Tbilisi run organized Askhi crossings that handle navigation and logistics.

A specific condition warning: avoid the route in rain or fresh snow. The Zubi side has clay soil that turns to deep, sticky mud, and the Kulbaki side has slippery slopes — both genuine slip and footing hazards when wet, not just discomforts. Combined with the navigation difficulty and the exposure on the plateau, this makes settled, dry weather important for a safe crossing. In winter, conditions make independent travel here genuinely hazardous.

Combining with other routes

Askhi rewards combination with the wider Lechkhumi–Samegrelo highlands. It can be linked with the Lebarde resort and the Tobavarchkhili lakes to the north (a longer multi-day traverse — the full Lebarde-to-Zubi route runs around five days), with the Akhalchala reserve, and — descending the western side — toward the Okatse and Martvili canyons and the Kinchkha area. For experienced trekkers, Askhi is a centerpiece for a longer western-Georgia mountain itinerary rather than only a standalone crossing.

Getting there and when to go

  • Location: Western Georgian highlands, Lechkhumi/Samegrelo border; eastern approach from Zubi, western from the Kulbaki area, both reachable from Tsageri (the Lechkhumi regional center), roughly 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi.
  • Getting there: Secondary mountain roads to the trailheads, conditions varying with season and weather; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable. Doable self-organized for experienced, well-equipped hikers, or as a guided crossing.
  • When to go: Roughly June–October, with July–September the most reliable for snow-free terrain, stable weather, and accessible roads. The plateau can hold snow into June; October brings the first autumn systems and cold, wet risk on the exposed limestone. Surface trekking is firmly a summer/early-autumn activity — winter is hazardous. (Caves are potentially accessible year-round for equipped speleologists.)
  • Difficulty: Demanding multi-day trek; significant elevation gain; unmarked; remote. For fit, experienced hikers — guide strongly recommended.

faq.title: What is the Askhi Massif?
faq.content: The Askhi Massif is a large limestone (karst) plateau in the western Georgian highlands, on the borders of the Lechkhumi and Samegrelo regions, rising to about 2,520 m at Gadrekili Peak. It's known for dramatic karst scenery — cliffs, sinkholes, and limestone pavement — for more than 80 documented caves (some containing permanent ice), and for vast panoramic views over much of western Georgia. Far less visited than the famous Greater Caucasus regions, it offers a genuinely remote wilderness experience, most often crossed on a two-day trek between the villages of Zubi and Kulbaki.

faq.title: How hard is the Askhi (Zubi–Kulbaki) trek?
faq.content: It's demanding — a route for fit, experienced hikers, not a casual walk. The full crossing is roughly 25–35 km, usually done over two days with a wild camp on the plateau, and the climb from Zubi alone gains on the order of 1,600 meters. The terrain isn't technical (no ropes or climbing), but it's remote, the trail marking is poor, surface water is scarce on the plateau, and there's no accommodation up top. You need good fitness, proper gear, the ability to carry enough water, and either solid navigation skills with GPS or — better — a local guide.

faq.title: Do you need a guide for the Askhi Massif?
faq.content: It's strongly recommended unless you're an experienced mountain hiker with good navigation tools. The plateau's karst terrain — sinkholes, limestone pavement, and open grassland with no obvious path — is genuinely easy to get lost on without detailed route knowledge or GPS tracks, and the remoteness means a navigational error has real consequences. Guiding services in the Lechkhumi region and Tbilisi run organized Askhi crossings that handle the navigation and logistics, which is the safest and most straightforward way to experience it for most visitors.

faq.title: When is the best time to hike Askhi, and what should I avoid?
faq.content: Roughly June to October, with July through September the most reliable for snow-free terrain, stable weather, and accessible roads; the plateau can hold snow into June, and October brings the first wet autumn weather. Crucially, avoid the route in rain or fresh snow: the Zubi side has clay soil that turns to deep mud and the Kulbaki side has slippery slopes, both real footing hazards when wet. Winter makes independent trekking here genuinely dangerous. Settled, dry summer weather is what you want.

faq.title: How do you get to the Askhi Massif?
faq.content: The trek is accessed from the Lechkhumi valley — the village of Zubi for the eastern start, the Kulbaki area for the western — both reached from Tsageri, the Lechkhumi regional center, about 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi. The approach roads are secondary mountain roads whose condition varies with the season, so a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps. Many trekkers combine Askhi with the wider Lechkhumi–Samegrelo highlands — the Lebarde resort and Tobavarchkhili lakes to the north, or a descent toward the Okatse and Martvili canyons to the west.

dekhviriFortress.heroTitle: Dekhviri Fortress: A Medieval Watchpost in Lechkhumi
dekhviriFortress.content:
Dekhviri Fortress is a medieval ruin in the Lechkhumi region of western Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — set on high ground above the divide between the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri river valleys, near the village of Dekhviri in the Tsageri municipality. It is one of the better-documented medieval fortifications in Lechkhumi — first appearing in written sources in the 17th century, though the structure likely predates those references — and its position above two major valley routes makes the military logic of its placement immediately legible: whoever held this point controlled movement through a substantial stretch of the regional mountain corridor. Today the ruins sit quietly in the forested Lechkhumi highlands, visited by very few travelers, offering the mix of genuine historical interest and panoramic valley views that mark the better of Georgia's little-known medieval sites. The surrounding area is also one of the country's most significant ancient metallurgical landscapes.

What is Dekhviri Fortress?

Dekhviri Fortress is a ruined medieval fortress in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia, near Dekhviri village in the Tsageri municipality, standing on the watershed between the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri rivers. A High Medieval structure first recorded in the 17th century, it controlled the valley routes of this contested mountain corridor and belonged to the Kingdom of Imereti before passing to the Principality of Samegrelo. The site sits within an area of deep history — the surrounding Dekhviri district was a major center of Bronze- and Iron-Age Colchian metalworking. Freely accessible and rarely visited, the ruins reward a short stop with their commanding views over the river valleys, best combined with the wider Lechkhumi circuit of wine villages, waterfalls, and lakes.

History and strategic importance

Lechkhumi held a specific, significant place in the medieval political geography of western Georgia. Lying between the Kingdom of Imereti to the south and the expanding Principality of Samegrelo to the west, with the high country of Svaneti reachable through the upper valleys to the north, it was both a transit corridor and a contested borderland whose control carried political and economic weight out of proportion to its small size. Dekhviri Fortress, commanding the watershed above which it sits, was part of the defensive infrastructure through which successive powers tried to hold this corridor.

The fortress is documented as belonging to the Kingdom of Imereti (with the rest of Lechkhumi) through roughly the 16th–18th centuries, held by the kings of Imereti, before passing in the 18th century to the Principality of Samegrelo — a shift that reflects the competition among the fragmented successor states of the unified Georgian kingdom over the highland territories between their cores. The political tug-of-war reached a documented climax in 1802, when King Solomon II of Imereti occupied the fortress, only for it to be returned to the Samegrelo principality a few years later by the Russian authorities then consolidating their control over the region. The physical structure — the wall placement, the towers' position relative to the valley approaches, the choice of elevation and terrain — reflects the medieval Caucasian defensive tradition, in which controlling high ground above valley routes was the fundamental principle of regional security.

The deeper past: Colchian metallurgy

The Dekhviri area's significance reaches far beyond the medieval fortress. The hill and the surrounding district have been the focus of archaeological investigation since the 1960s–70s, resumed in the 2010s, and the findings place this landscape among the most important ancient metallurgical centers in Georgia. Excavations indicate settlement here from the late 2nd millennium and through the 1st millennium BC — the Late Bronze and Early Iron Age — and reveal that the people of the Tskheta-Dekhviri area were engaged in copper and bronze production, part of the celebrated Colchian bronze culture of ancient western Georgia (the legendary Colchis of the Golden Fleece). Characteristic Colchian bronze objects and metalwork hoards from the area are now held in the municipal museum in Tsageri. The medieval fortress, in other words, is a relatively recent addition to a landscape that had been a focus of organized human activity — and skilled metalworking — for well over two millennia before it was built. (The fortress site itself has been under recent archaeological study, and Dekhviri has been recognized for national heritage protection.)

This deep agricultural and craft continuity resonates with the region's living culture today: Lechkhumi is renowned for its wines — the Tvishi appellation and the rare Usakhelouri grape among them — an expression of an agricultural tradition with very deep roots in this specific landscape.

The ruins

The remains at Dekhviri consist of surviving stone walls, defensive structures, and elevated lookout positions that have withstood time, disuse, and the slow encroachment of the forest. The condition varies across the site — sections where the wall was heaviest and most carefully built retain meaningful height and let the original defensive circuit be read, while other areas have been reduced to lower courses or scattered stone. The overall state is one of partial survival rather than either full preservation or total loss, giving the ruins the atmospheric quality of a medieval site part-way through its return to nature.

Walking the ruins gives a direct sense of the site's medieval defensive logic — the walls placed in relation to the valley approaches below, the lookout positions from which the terrain was watched, the way the builders exploited the natural topography. And the elevated position that made the fortress valuable then is still the most rewarding feature of a visit now: the views over the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri valleys and the forested Lechkhumi landscape beyond give a geographical understanding of this corner of western Georgia that the valley floor cannot.

The setting

The forested mountain terrain around Dekhviri is characteristic of the Lechkhumi highlands — dense mixed deciduous forest (chestnut, oak, hornbeam, maple) on the slopes, the rivers audible if not always visible from the fortress, and the green enclosure and mountain quiet that define the region for those who spend time in it. The views from the fortress over the river junction below connect the medieval military logic of the site to the natural terrain in a way that's immediately, intuitively clear — you can see, standing here, exactly why someone built a stronghold on this spot.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Near Dekhviri village, Tsageri municipality, Lechkhumi (Racha-Lechkhumi and Kvemo Svaneti region), western Georgia; on the divide above the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri valleys.
  • Getting there: Reached from Tsageri (the Lechkhumi regional center, ~5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi) via the Tskhenistskali valley road network. Access roads are secondary mountain roads whose condition varies with season and weather; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable, especially after rain. A short footpath leads to the ruins.
  • Time needed: About 20–40 minutes for the surviving ruins and the viewpoints.
  • Access: Freely accessible, no entrance fee or visitor infrastructure.
  • Combine with: The Lechkhumi circuit — the Tvishi wine area, the region's waterfalls (Ghvirishi, Verdzistava), the Kulbaki lakes, and the Tsageri museum (which holds the area's Colchian bronze finds) — as part of a slow, unhurried regional exploration.

faq.title: What is Dekhviri Fortress?
faq.content: Dekhviri Fortress is a ruined medieval fortress in the Lechkhumi highlands of western Georgia, near Dekhviri village in the Tsageri municipality, standing on the watershed between the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri rivers. A High Medieval structure first recorded in written sources in the 17th century, it controlled the valley routes of this contested mountain corridor. It belonged to the Kingdom of Imereti before passing to the Principality of Samegrelo. Freely accessible and rarely visited, the ruins reward a short stop with commanding views over the river valleys.

faq.title: Why was Dekhviri Fortress important?
faq.content: Lechkhumi sat between the Kingdom of Imereti, the Principality of Samegrelo, and the highlands of Svaneti — a small but strategically significant contested corridor — and Dekhviri commanded the watershed above the Tskhenistskali and Lajanuri valleys, controlling movement through a substantial part of it. It was held by the kings of Imereti through roughly the 16th–18th centuries, then passed to Samegrelo. In a documented episode in 1802, King Solomon II of Imereti occupied the fortress, but the Russian authorities soon returned it to the Samegrelo principality — a small window onto the turbulent politics of western Georgia in that era.

faq.title: Is there more to the Dekhviri area than the fortress?
faq.content: Yes — and it's genuinely significant. The Dekhviri district is one of Georgia's important ancient metallurgical landscapes: archaeological work (from the 1960s–70s and resumed in the 2010s) shows settlement here through the Late Bronze and Iron Ages, with evidence of copper and bronze production belonging to the famous Colchian bronze culture of ancient western Georgia — the legendary land of Colchis and the Golden Fleece. Characteristic Colchian bronze finds from the area are kept in the museum in Tsageri. So the medieval fortress sits atop a landscape that was a center of skilled metalworking more than two thousand years before it was built.

faq.title: Is Dekhviri Fortress worth visiting?
faq.content: It's a modest ruin rather than a dramatic monument, so it's best seen as a short, atmospheric stop within a wider Lechkhumi trip rather than a destination in itself. Its appeal is twofold: the genuine historical interest of a well-documented medieval fortress in a contested borderland, and the excellent views over the river valleys from its commanding position — you can see at a glance why it was built here. For travelers drawn to off-the-beaten-path history and quiet mountain landscapes, it's a rewarding 20–40-minute stop, especially combined with the region's wine, waterfalls, and the Colchian-bronze collection in Tsageri.

faq.title: How do you get to Dekhviri Fortress?
faq.content: It's near Dekhviri village in the Tsageri municipality of Lechkhumi, reached from Tsageri (the regional center, about 5 hours from Tbilisi via Kutaisi) along the Tskhenistskali valley roads, with a short footpath up to the ruins. The access roads are secondary mountain roads whose condition varies with the season and recent weather, so a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance is advisable, especially after rain. The site is free and has no visitor infrastructure. It pairs naturally with the rest of the Lechkhumi circuit — the Tvishi wine villages, the area's waterfalls, the Kulbaki lakes, and the Tsageri museum.

tsughrughasheniChurch.heroTitle: Tsughrughasheni Church: A Carved Jewel Near Bolnisi
tsughrughasheniChurch.content:
Tsughrughasheni is an early-13th-century church in the Kvemo Kartli region of southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — standing in quiet rural country about 2 kilometers from the famous Bolnisi Sioni Cathedral, on the right bank of the Bolnisistskali River. It is one of the finest examples of ornamental stone carving in Georgian church architecture, its surfaces — and above all its remarkable tall, narrow dome — covered in relief decoration of a density and refinement that place it among the more accomplished achievements of medieval Georgian craftsmanship. Yet it remains little known, even among travelers who care about such things. For anyone exploring the Bolnisi area — combining Bolnisi Sioni, the Dmanisi archaeological site, and the town's German heritage — Tsughrughasheni adds an artistic dimension none of the others offers in such concentrated form.

What is Tsughrughasheni Church?

Tsughrughasheni is a richly carved early-13th-century Georgian Orthodox church about 2 km from Bolnisi Sioni, in the Bolnisi district of Kvemo Kartli, southern Georgia. Built in 1212–1222 during the reign of King George IV "Lasha" (Queen Tamar's son), at the very end of the Georgian Golden Age, it's a domed church famous for its unusually tall, slender drum and the dense ornamental stone carving around the dome and windows — comparable to celebrated carved churches like Nikortsminda, Betania, and Pitareti, though smaller and far less visited. Quiet, rural, and freely accessible, it's the artistic highlight of the Bolnisi cluster and pairs naturally with Bolnisi Sioni on a Kvemo Kartli day from Tbilisi.

Architecture and carving

Tsughrughasheni is a central-domed church of moderate scale, built in the mature Georgian ecclesiastical tradition of the early 13th century and closely related in style to the great Golden-Age churches of Betania, Kvatakhevi, and Pitareti — though smaller than those. Its single most distinctive feature is its dome: an unusually tall, slender drum, taller in proportion than is typical for Georgia, rising to give the whole building a vertical, reaching, almost weightless quality. The drum is pierced by ten windows, and it is here, and around the window frames, that the church's celebrated decoration is concentrated.

The carving is what makes Tsughrughasheni remarkable. The drum and the window surrounds are covered in relief ornament of exceptional refinement — cross motifs of varying complexity, geometric interlacing, foliate borders, and symbolic carvings — layered into bands of decoration that give each surface a visual depth changing with the angle of light. The window surrounds in particular are framed by multiple bands of carved ornament — geometric, botanical, and symbolic motifs combined with both technical skill and sophisticated decorative thinking. The precision of the geometric patterns, the crispness of the relief where the stone has been protected from weathering, and the consistency of standard across the surfaces reflect a team of highly skilled carvers working to a coherent design. The building is also notable for its stone itself — worked from blocks of gold-toned, dark-purple, and bluish stone that give the exterior a subtle polychrome warmth.

Medieval Georgian stone carving at its best is a tradition of real sophistication, and Tsughrughasheni is among its finer surviving expressions — its ornamental program is regularly compared to the most celebrated carved facades in the country, including Nikortsminda in Racha, though Tsughrughasheni is far less widely known. The condition of the carving varies with exposure — sheltered passages keep detail the more weathered surfaces have partly lost — but enough survives in readable condition to grasp the full scope of the original program. The way to experience it is slowly: circling the exterior, stopping to read individual passages of carving at close range, then stepping back to take in the composition of each surface and the soaring drum as a whole.

History

The church was built in 1212–1222, during the reign of King George IV, known as Lasha-Giorgi — the son and successor of Queen Tamar — at the very close of Georgia's Golden Age, the brilliant period of unified-kingdom power and cultural confidence built under David the Builder and Tamar. The investment in ornamental carving of this quality reflects the resources and ambitions of that moment: it required both wealthy royal patronage and access to highly skilled craftsmen, and its existence in the relatively provincial landscape of Kvemo Kartli shows that the cultural reach of the Golden Age extended well beyond the royal capitals and major monasteries. It was built, in effect, just as that golden moment was ending — the Mongol invasions that would transform the whole Caucasus arrived during and just after Lasha-Giorgi's reign.

Unusually for a regional church, something of its making is recorded: inscriptions on the building name King Giorgi and a builder, Hasan, son of Arsen — a rare named glimpse of the craftsman behind a medieval Georgian church. (The church is dedicated to the Cross of St. George.) Beyond this, the fuller circumstances — the precise patronage, the workshop, the building's social role — are not documented in surviving sources, the common situation for monuments significant at a regional rather than national level. What the building communicates, in the language of its carved surfaces, is a confident, highly developed artistic tradition applied with skill and ambition to a structure its builders clearly meant to be outstanding.

The interior and setting

The interior is more modest than the exterior leads you to expect — a characteristic of this kind of Georgian ornamental church, where the decorative investment went into the exterior surfaces seen by the wider community rather than the interior reserved for liturgy. The interior was once painted, but the frescoes have been badly damaged over the centuries, and what remains is a well-proportioned domed space without the elaboration that makes the exterior so striking. It serves mainly to confirm that the building is a complete, coherent architectural work rather than only a carrier of ornamental surface.

The rural setting suits both the church's scale and the kind of attentive looking the carving rewards. It stands on a hillside slope in the open agricultural landscape of Kvemo Kartli, within a small walled enclosure with monastery buildings behind it, the surrounding countryside quiet in the way that Georgian rural sites off the main tourist routes tend to be. The near-certain absence of other visitors — Tsughrughasheni keeps a very low profile in Georgia's heritage landscape — lets you examine the carving at whatever pace suits you, and gives the visit an intimacy that the more celebrated carved churches of Georgia rarely allow.

Getting there and visiting

  • Location: Bolnisi district, Kvemo Kartli region, southern Georgia, ~2 km from Bolnisi Sioni Cathedral, on the right bank of the Bolnisistskali River; ~70 km (1.5 hrs) south of Tbilisi.
  • Getting there: By car along rural roads; the approach is straightforward but not extensively signposted, so GPS or local knowledge helps for the final stretch. Almost always combined with Bolnisi Sioni (just 2 km away).
  • Time needed: About 20–30 minutes for a thorough look at the carving, interior, and setting — longer if the detail absorbs you.
  • Access: Freely accessible, unstaffed, no visitor infrastructure. An active church — modest dress is appropriate (shoulders and knees covered).
  • Combine with: Bolnisi Sioni, the Bolnisi Museum, the German colonial town, and Dmanisi, on a Kvemo Kartli day.

faq.title: What is Tsughrughasheni Church?
faq.content: Tsughrughasheni is a richly carved early-13th-century Georgian Orthodox church in the Bolnisi district of Kvemo Kartli, southern Georgia, about 2 km from the famous Bolnisi Sioni Cathedral. Built in 1212–1222 during the reign of King George IV "Lasha" (Queen Tamar's son), it's a domed church best known for its unusually tall, slender drum and the dense, refined stone carving around the dome and windows — among the finest in Georgia, comparable to celebrated churches like Nikortsminda and Betania.
