Hiking in the surrounding alpine terrain — the open meadows and ridgelines above the settlement opening to ever-wider views over Guria and, on clear days, the distant Black Sea and the Greater Caucasus — is the most natural activity, with routes from short walks around the settlement to full-day ridge traverses that need proper mountain preparation.

Horseback riding is available through local providers and gives a different pace and perspective than walking; the traditional role of horses in the highland pastoral economy here gives it an authenticity that organized riding elsewhere can lack — and Gurian horsemanship is genuinely renowned (Gurian riders were even recruited into Buffalo Bill's Wild West Show in the 1880s). That heritage peaks each year on August 19, when Bakhmaro hosts a famous horse race tied to the Orthodox feast of the Transfiguration — the settlement's biggest event, drawing riders and crowds. For photographers, the changing light, the variable cloud sea, and the cottages-against-landscape compositions give material that rewards sustained engagement rather than a single quick visit.

Access

The approach from the Chokhatauri area climbs a mountain road from the lower Gurian terrain through forested slopes to the settlement. The road has been progressively improved and much of it is now paved, though it remains long and winding, and the condition of any remaining rough sections varies with recent weather; in dry summer conditions an ordinary car generally manages the drive, but after heavy rain a higher-clearance or four-wheel-drive vehicle is the safer choice. Allow roughly one and a half to two hours from Chokhatauri depending on conditions, and around two hours from Ozurgeti, the regional center of Guria. The road is generally open only in the summer season; snow makes it impassable from roughly November onward.

Chokhatauri is reachable from Batumi in around an hour and a half, and from Tbilisi in about four to four and a half hours via the main east–west highway (note that some private-transfer services quote three to four hours total from Batumi door-to-door, given the mountain section). Shared minibuses (marshrutkas) run up to Bakhmaro in the summer high season, roughly mid-June to the end of September. Bakhmaro is most naturally approached as part of a Guria itinerary — taking in Ozurgeti, the Shemokmedi Monastery, and the Gomismta mountain — or as an overnight mountain destination for travelers based in Batumi who want a highland experience within a manageable drive of the coast.

When to visit

June through September is the season, with July and August the most active months, when the seasonal community is in residence and the guesthouses are fully operating. Late August and September are especially rewarding — the late-summer and early-autumn conditions produce some of the most dramatic cloud formations of the year, with fewer visitors than the peak July weeks (and the August 19 horse race falls in this window). The road typically becomes impassable with snow from November onward, and the settlement is largely empty through winter. Early June can still see the road affected by the last of the snowmelt depending on the year, so confirming road conditions before an early-season visit is wise.

Practical information

  • Location: Bakhmaro, Chokhatauri Municipality, Guria, western Georgia; on the Meskheti range at roughly 2,000–2,050 m, on the Bakhvistskali River.
  • Getting there: By car, taxi/private transfer, or summer marshrutka, up the mountain road from Chokhatauri (about 1.5–2 hours); Chokhatauri is ~1.5 hours from Batumi, ~4–4.5 hours from Tbilisi. Summer access only.
  • When: June–September (July–August busiest; late August–September best for clouds and the horse race).
  • Stay overnight: Essential to catch the dawn sea of clouds; book guesthouses or a cottage ahead in peak season.
  • Know before you go: Basic infrastructure (limited water, sometimes intermittent electricity); bring layers (cool even in summer) and confirm road conditions, especially early/late season.

faq.title: What is Bakhmaro?
faq.content: Bakhmaro is a high-altitude summer resort in the Guria region of western Georgia, at roughly 2,000–2,050 meters in the Meskheti mountains above Chokhatauri. It's the country's highest alpine climatic (health) resort, known since the late 19th century for clean air that mixes Black Sea and mountain qualities, and famous today for its "sea of clouds" — a cloud layer that fills the valleys below the settlement at dawn and dusk. Its iconic wooden cottages stand on stilts across the alpine meadows.

faq.title: What is the "sea of clouds" at Bakhmaro?
faq.content: It's a cloud layer that settles into the valleys below Bakhmaro while the settlement stays clear above it, so you look out over a white "sea" of cloud. It forms because humid air from the nearby Black Sea, the elevation difference, and temperature inversions let cloud stabilize at valley level. It's most likely and most dramatic in the early morning and around sunset, especially in the days after rain, which is why staying overnight is the way to experience it — Bakhmaro is even nicknamed the "Kingdom of Clouds."

faq.title: How do you get to Bakhmaro, and do you need a 4x4?
faq.content: Bakhmaro is reached by a long, winding mountain road up from Chokhatauri (about 1.5–2 hours), which is itself about 1.5 hours from Batumi and 4–4.5 hours from Tbilisi. The road has been much improved and is largely paved, so in dry summer conditions an ordinary car can usually make it; after heavy rain a higher-clearance or 4x4 vehicle is safer. Summer marshrutkas also run. Access is essentially summer-only — snow closes the road from around November.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Bakhmaro?
faq.content: The season is June through September. July and August are busiest, with the seasonal community in residence and all guesthouses open. Late August and September are arguably best — the cloud formations are at their most dramatic and there are fewer visitors, and the famous Bakhmaro horse race takes place on August 19 (tied to the Orthodox Transfiguration feast). Outside summer the road is generally snowbound and the settlement largely empty.

faq.title: Where do you stay in Bakhmaro and what should you know?
faq.content: Accommodation is in family guesthouses or rented wooden cottages — staying overnight is essential to catch the dawn sea of clouds, and you should book ahead in peak season. The food is hearty Gurian cooking (spicy by Georgian standards, with local khachapuri and mountain dairy). Be aware the infrastructure is basic: water supply is limited and electricity can be intermittent, and even in summer it's cool at altitude, so bring warm layers. It's part of the rustic charm, but worth planning for.

petraFortress.heroTitle: Petra Fortress: Justinian's Byzantine Stronghold on the Adjara Coast
petraFortress.content:
Petra Fortress occupies a rocky promontory above the Black Sea coast about 25 kilometers north of Batumi, near the village of Tsikhisdziri, in Adjara in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Its ruined walls are visible from the coastal road below, and its elevated position commands views over the sea and the coastline in both directions. It's a less-visited and considerably less-developed site than Gonio to the south, and the combination of the dramatic natural setting, the genuine antiquity of the ruins, and the relative absence of organized tourism gives it a character that appeals to travelers who like their historical sites with some roughness.

What is Petra Fortress?

Petra Fortress is a 6th-century Byzantine fortress-city at Tsikhisdziri, on the Black Sea coast about 25 km north of Batumi in Adjara, Georgia. Built under Emperor Justinian I (and named "Petra," Greek for "rock"), it guarded the southern approach to the kingdom of Lazica and became a fiercely contested prize in the Lazic War between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia — the historian Procopius describes its sieges in detail. Today it's a ruined hilltop archaeological site with standing Byzantine walls, reached by a short steep walk, with sweeping sea views. It's a quieter, wilder counterpart to the Roman fortress at Gonio.

A fortress built by Justinian

The fortress was built in the 6th century during the reign of the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I, as part of the empire's effort to consolidate control over the eastern Black Sea coast and its trade routes during a period of intense competition with the Sassanid Persian Empire. It's first referred to in 535, in Justinian's own legal decrees, and was developed from an earlier small Roman fort into a fortified city named, in the emperor's honor, Petra Pia Justiniana. The site — a rocky headland rising steeply from the sea, with natural cliffs defending most sides and only a narrow neck of land connecting it to the mainland — was chosen with the eye for defensible topography that marks the best Byzantine military architecture of the period. The name Petra, meaning "rock" in Greek, describes the site's essential character directly and has been in use since antiquity.

According to the Byzantine historian Procopius, who documented Justinian's wars in detail, the fortress was founded through the efforts of the Roman official John Tzibus, who then used it to control imports into Lazica — monopolizing access to luxury goods and to the much-needed salt trade. That commercial stranglehold caused such resentment among the local Laz that it helped trigger the conflict to come.

The Lazic War

Petra's strategic position made it one of the most contested sites on the eastern Black Sea coast in the 6th century. It became a central prize in the prolonged conflict known as the Lazic War (roughly 541–562) — the struggle between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia for control of the Kingdom of Lazica, the political entity that occupied much of what is now western Georgia. The fortress changed hands more than once: the Persians took it in 541, the Byzantines besieged it unsuccessfully in 549, and after a famous and brutal siege in 550–551 — which Procopius described in vivid detail — the Byzantines retook it and then deliberately demolished it themselves, to prevent the Persians from ever using it again. The detailed presence of Petra in the contemporary historical record, through Procopius and Justinian's own decrees, gives it a documented importance that most sites of comparable size in the Caucasus lack.

The site was held, damaged, and partly rebuilt by various powers through the medieval period (it served for a time as a regional church center under Constantinople), and Ottoman forces controlled it during the centuries of Ottoman presence in Adjara before Russian annexation in the 19th century. The ruins visible today represent the accumulated construction of these successive occupations rather than a single campaign. Excavations around the site have uncovered far more than the citadel walls — 4th-century baths, an early Byzantine villa, an early church, and an extensive cemetery with gold, silver, and bronze grave goods — and Petra is now protected as an archaeological museum-reserve.

The site and the walk

Reaching the fortress requires a short but fairly steep uphill walk from the road below, gaining height quickly through the subtropical scrub and forest of the Adjarian coastal zone. The effort is modest — the ascent takes fifteen to twenty minutes for most visitors — but enough that arriving at the walls feels like having earned the views that open up at the top.

The ruins are substantial in places and fragmentary in others, the uneven preservation reflecting both the varying quality of construction across building periods and the centuries of weathering and vegetation since the site's decline. Sections of the original Byzantine curtain wall stand to considerable height, and the corner towers that defined the perimeter are identifiable even where the masonry has partly collapsed. The interior is an open space of terraced ground, grass and wildflowers, with scattered stone blocks and foundation remains throughout.

The views are the most immediately rewarding feature. The headland gives an unobstructed panorama over the Black Sea to the west, with the coastline visible both north and south from the elevated walls. The Batumi skyline, with its cluster of modern towers, is visible to the south on clear days. The forested Adjarian highlands rise immediately inland, and the contrast between the dark green of the slopes and the blue of the sea gives the view a richness that flat coastal sites can't match.

Getting there

The fortress is near the village of Tsikhisdziri, about 25 kilometers north of Batumi along the coastal road; the drive takes around 25 to 30 minutes. It's visible from the road, and the path to the ruins begins near a small parking area at the base of the promontory. Marshrutkas running the coastal road between Batumi and Kobuleti pass Tsikhisdziri, so the site is reachable without a car, though the stop leaves a short walk to the fortress base. (Tsikhisdziri also has well-regarded beaches below the headland, so the fortress combines easily with time by the sea.)

Practical information

  • Location: Tsikhisdziri village, Adjara, about 25 km (25–30 minutes) north of Batumi on the coastal road, between Batumi and Kobuleti.
  • Getting there: By car, taxi, or coastal-road marshrutka (Batumi–Kobuleti); a short steep walk (about 15–20 minutes) up to the ruins from the parking area at the base.
  • Hours/Admission: Open daily in daylight hours; an entrance fee is payable at the site (it's a managed archaeological museum-reserve).
  • Best to know: Wear proper footwear for the uphill path; the site is exposed, so bring sun protection. Pairs well with the Tsikhisdziri beaches or with Gonio Fortress (south of Batumi) for a Byzantine-and-Roman fortress pairing.

faq.title: What is Petra Fortress?
faq.content: Petra Fortress is a 6th-century Byzantine fortress-city at Tsikhisdziri, on the Black Sea coast about 25 km north of Batumi in Adjara, Georgia. Built under Emperor Justinian I and named "Petra" (Greek for "rock"), it guarded the southern approach to the Kingdom of Lazica and became a fiercely contested site in the Lazic War between Byzantium and Sassanid Persia. Today it's a ruined hilltop archaeological site with standing Byzantine walls and sweeping sea views, reached by a short steep walk.

faq.title: Who built Petra Fortress and when?
faq.content: It was built in the 6th century under the Byzantine Emperor Justinian I — first mentioned in 535 — developed from an earlier small Roman fort into a fortified city named Petra Pia Justiniana in his honor. The contemporary historian Procopius records that it was founded through the imperial official John Tzibus, who used it to control trade into Lazica. Its purpose was to anchor Byzantine power on the eastern Black Sea coast against Sassanid Persia.

faq.title: Why was Petra important in the Lazic War?
faq.content: Petra guarded the coastal southern gateway into Lazica (western Georgia), so whoever held it controlled access to the kingdom. During the Lazic War (about 541–562) it changed hands repeatedly: the Persians seized it in 541, the Byzantines failed to retake it in 549, and after a brutal siege in 550–551 — described in detail by Procopius — the Byzantines recaptured it and then demolished it themselves so the Persians couldn't use it again. That documented role makes it one of the best-recorded ancient sites in the region.

faq.title: How do you get to Petra Fortress from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 25–30 minutes by car or taxi north along the coastal road, near Tsikhisdziri between Batumi and Kobuleti. Marshrutkas on the Batumi–Kobuleti coastal route pass Tsikhisdziri, so you can reach it without a car, with a short walk to the base. From there it's a fairly steep 15–20 minute climb up to the ruins. Tsikhisdziri also has good beaches, so it's easy to combine the fortress with time by the sea.

faq.title: Is Petra Fortress worth visiting?
faq.content: For travelers interested in Byzantine and ancient history, yes — it has genuine standing 6th-century walls, a remarkable documented history from the Lazic War, and one of the best coastal panoramas in the area. It's wilder and less developed than Gonio, so expect ruins and a short climb rather than a polished attraction. If you want a quiet, atmospheric historical site with a view and a real story behind it, it rewards the visit; the two fortresses (Petra north, Gonio south) make a natural Byzantine-and-Roman pairing.

mtiralaNationalPark.heroTitle: Mtirala National Park: Georgia's Colchic Rainforest near Batumi
mtiralaNationalPark.content:
Mtirala National Park occupies a section of the Adjarian highlands about 25 kilometers northeast of Batumi, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus, in the valley system of the Chakvistskali River and its tributaries. The park's name means "crying" in Georgian — a reference to the rainfall that defines this corner of the Caucasus more than any other feature. Mtirala is one of the wettest places in Georgia, receiving somewhere between roughly 2,500 and 4,500 millimeters of precipitation a year depending on elevation, and the result of that rainfall is a forest of extraordinary density and botanical richness — one of the finest surviving examples of Colchic rainforest in the entire Caucasus.

What is Mtirala National Park?

Mtirala National Park is a Colchic rainforest park in Adjara, Georgia, about 25 km northeast of Batumi, established in 2006 and covering roughly 15,700 hectares. Its name means "crying," after the very high rainfall (up to ~4,500 mm a year) that makes it one of the wettest places in the country and sustains a lush, moss-draped forest. Since 2021 it has been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands." From the visitor center at Chakvistavi, marked trails lead to waterfalls, a turquoise river lake, rope crossings, and a zipline. It's a popular half-day or day trip from Batumi.

The Colchic rainforest

The Colchic forest — the ancient subtropical forest type that once covered much of the western Caucasus lowlands and foothills — has been reduced to scattered remnants by agriculture and development across most of its former range, and the examples preserved within Mtirala are among the most intact and ecologically significant survivors. The vegetation has a lushness and layered complexity that visitors used to temperate European forests find immediately striking: a canopy of oriental beech, sweet chestnut, and alder above; a dense mid-story of rhododendron and cherry laurel; the ground covered in mosses and ferns; and nearly every surface — rocks, fallen logs, the lower trunks of trees — draped in green, with lianas threading through it all. In wet conditions, which are frequent, the forest produces a mist that hangs between the trees and gives the park the atmospheric quality its name describes.

In recognition of these relict forests and their biodiversity, Mtirala was inscribed in 2021 — together with the Kintrishi, Kobuleti, and Kolkheti protected areas — as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands."

Wildlife and ecology

Mtirala's biodiversity reflects its combination of high rainfall, varied topography, and relative ecological intactness. The forest supports brown bear, wolf, lynx, wild boar, and roe deer, along with a rich bird community that includes old-growth Colchic-forest species (almost all of Europe's woodpecker species occur here, alongside booted eagle, eagle owl, and golden oriole). As you'd expect in one of the most humid parts of the country, the amphibian life is a particular highlight — the Caucasian salamander, Caucasian toad, and others thrive in the moist environment — and the cold streams carry trout. The botanical diversity is considerable, with several Colchic endemic species and high densities of Caucasian plants in the favorable growing conditions; in May and late September, flowering rhododendrons add pink and yellow to the forest.

The park was established in 2006 and covers roughly 15,700 hectares (spanning the Kobuleti, Khelvachauri, and Keda municipalities), a contiguous area large enough to support viable wildlife populations and maintain the ecological processes that sustain the Colchic forest. Buffer zones around the protected core allow limited sustainable use while preserving the integrity of the central conservation area.

Hiking and activities

The park has a network of marked trails from the main entrance near the village of Chakvistavi, where the visitor center and basic facilities are. The trails range from short walks of an hour or two for casual visitors to longer routes of four to six hours that go deeper into the forest to the more remote waterfalls and viewpoints. The main trails pass through dense forest, crossing streams on wooden bridges and following paths that are in many places more like tunnels through the vegetation than open tracks.

The park's waterfalls are among its most visited features — the main one, the Tsablnari (Tsalbnari) waterfall, is reached within about an hour of the entrance, and near it a small turquoise, trout-rich lake fed by the cold river is one of the prettiest spots in the park (and a swimming spot for the hardy). The combination of falling water, moss-covered rocks, and surrounding forest creates environments of real beauty, and the sound of moving water is a near-constant presence across the trail network.

Rope crossings over the wider sections of the river add a mild adventure element that's more about fun than challenge — straightforward for most visitors, but a kinetic engagement with the landscape the walking trails alone don't offer. There's also an old mechanical cable car over the Chakvistskali (a small fee) and a zipline near the visitor center giving a brief aerial perspective on the canopy. These elements are popular with families and give a Mtirala visit a slightly different character from a purely contemplative nature walk. Horse riding and canyoning are also available in season.

Practical considerations

Mtirala's rainfall means the park is genuinely wet for much of the year, and visitors should prepare accordingly whatever the season. Waterproof footwear is strongly recommended — trails are frequently muddy and stream crossings involve some contact with water even on bridges — and a light waterproof layer is worth carrying year-round, since showers can arrive quickly even on clear-starting days. Insect repellent helps in the warmer months.

The park is coolest and wettest in spring and early summer, when the forest is at its most intensely green and the waterfalls at their most powerful. Summer brings warmer temperatures and the most visitors, but the forest shade keeps the interior considerably cooler than the coastal city below — a welcome escape from the summer heat. Autumn is the most visually dramatic, when the deciduous trees layer color through the mostly evergreen canopy. Winter is quiet, with the lower trails accessible though the higher sections may be snow-covered.

Getting there

The park is about 30 to 40 minutes from Batumi by car, heading northeast through Chakvi and on to the visitor center at Chakvistavi. Organized tours from Batumi frequently include Mtirala, often combined with the Makhuntseti Waterfall and Queen Tamar Bridge in a half- or full-day excursion. Independent access by public transport is limited (there are infrequent minibuses from the Old Batumi bus station, and you can take a minibus to Chakvi and a taxi for the last ~15 km), so a private vehicle or taxi is the most practical option for most visitors.

Practical information

  • Location: Chakvistavi village, Adjara, about 25 km (30–40 minutes) northeast of Batumi; visitor center at the main entrance.
  • Hours/Admission: Open daily; the visitor center operates in daylight hours. Park entry is free; some organized activities (zipline, cable car, guided/adventure options) carry a separate fee.
  • Best time: April through October for hiking and trail access; spring and autumn are the most rewarding.
  • Bring: Waterproof footwear and a light rain layer (any season), insect repellent (summer); it's cooler than the coast.
  • Status: Part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands" (since 2021).

faq.title: What is Mtirala National Park?
faq.content: Mtirala National Park is a Colchic rainforest park in Adjara, Georgia, about 25 km northeast of Batumi, established in 2006 and covering roughly 15,700 hectares. Its name means "crying," after the very high rainfall — up to around 4,500 mm a year, among the wettest in the country — that sustains a dense, moss-draped forest. Since 2021 it's been part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands." From the Chakvistavi visitor center, trails lead to waterfalls, a turquoise river lake, rope crossings, and a zipline.

faq.title: Why is it called Mtirala ("crying")?
faq.content: The name refers to the extraordinary rainfall. Mtirala — and Mount Mtirala on its territory — means "crying" in Georgian, because the area is almost constantly wet: it's one of the rainiest places in Georgia (and reputedly in this part of Europe), receiving up to about 4,500 mm of precipitation a year. That rain is exactly what creates the park's lush, mist-filled Colchic rainforest, so the name captures both the climate and the character of the place.

faq.title: What is there to do in Mtirala National Park?
faq.content: Mainly hiking through dense rainforest on marked trails, from short one-to-two-hour walks to longer four-to-six-hour routes. The highlights are the Tsablnari waterfall and the small turquoise, trout-rich lake near it. There are also rope river crossings, an old cable car over the river, and a zipline near the visitor center, plus horse riding and canyoning in season — so it suits both contemplative nature walks and families wanting a bit of adventure. Pack for rain and mud whatever the forecast.

faq.title: How do you get to Mtirala from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 30–40 minutes by car, northeast through Chakvi to the visitor center at Chakvistavi. It's frequently included in organized day tours from Batumi, often paired with the Makhuntseti Waterfall and Queen Tamar Bridge. Public transport is limited — there are infrequent minibuses, or you can take one to Chakvi and a taxi for the last stretch — so a car, taxi, or tour is usually easiest.

faq.title: How is Mtirala different from Machakhela National Park?
faq.content: Both protect the same Colchic rainforest and are part of the same UNESCO listing, but they feel different. Mtirala is the classic rainforest experience — a wetter, more enclosed park focused on the forest, waterfalls, and trails, with developed visitor facilities and activities (zipline, cable car), closer to Batumi and more visited. Machakhela is a wider inhabited valley where villages and a famous gunsmith heritage are as much the draw as the forest, and it's far quieter. For waterfalls and rainforest walks, Mtirala; for nature plus living culture and near-solitude, Machakhela.

makhuntsetiWaterfall.heroTitle: Makhuntseti Waterfall & Queen Tamar Bridge: An Adjara Half-Day from Batumi
makhuntsetiWaterfall.content:
The Makhuntseti Waterfall and the Queen Tamar Bridge sit in the Acharistskali River valley in the Adjarian highlands, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus, about 30–35 kilometers from Batumi along a road that climbs steadily into the forested mountain terrain above the coastal city. They're consistently among the most visited natural and historical sites in Adjara, and the combination — a powerful waterfall reached by a short walk from the road, and a beautifully preserved medieval stone bridge a few minutes away — makes the excursion one of the most rewarding half-day trips from Batumi.

What are the Makhuntseti Waterfall and Queen Tamar Bridge?

Makhuntseti, about 30–35 km from Batumi in the Adjarian highlands, pairs two sights a short walk apart: the Makhuntseti Waterfall — around 30 meters high, one of the tallest in Adjara, with a pool you can swim in during summer — and the Queen Tamar Bridge, a single-arch medieval stone bridge (12th–13th century) over the Acharistskali River. Both are free (small parking fee), reached in about 40 minutes by car, and easily combined into a half-day trip, often with the nearby Mirveti Waterfall and the river confluence. It's one of the most popular excursions from Batumi.

Getting there: the drive into the highlands

The drive from Batumi to Makhuntseti takes around forty minutes and is itself part of the experience. The road leaves the subtropical lowlands of the coast and climbs into dense mixed forest, the valley narrowing as the elevation rises and the mountain character of Adjara becoming progressively more evident. Tea plantations cover the lower hillsides on the approach — a reminder that this region produced the majority of Georgia's domestic tea. By the time the road reaches the Makhuntseti area, the valley has the enclosed, forested quality of the Adjarian highlands that makes this part of Georgia visually distinct from the open agricultural landscapes of the east. Along the way, the confluence of the Chorokhi and Acharistskali rivers is a noted sight, where the two differently-colored currents meet without immediately mixing.

The waterfall

The Makhuntseti Waterfall drops around 30 meters into a pool at its base — one of the highest waterfalls in Adjara (height estimates vary, with some sources putting it higher). It falls with enough volume year-round to throw up a constant spray that noticeably cools the surrounding air. The falls are close to the road and reached by a short walk of only a few minutes from the parking area, making it one of the most easily reached waterfalls of its scale in western Georgia; the path passes through riverside vegetation to a rocky viewing area beside the pool where the full height is visible.

In spring and early summer, when snowmelt from the higher elevations adds to the flow, the waterfall is at its most powerful and the spray considerable. In summer the pool at the base is warm enough that people swim in it (and the brave stand under the falling water), and the mix of cool mist above and warmer pool below makes it a popular spot for that in July and August. Autumn brings the most photogenic conditions, with the surrounding forest turning color and lower visitor numbers giving the site a quietness the peak months can't offer.

A small market of local vendors typically operates near the parking area in the warmer months, selling honey, churchkhela, homemade wine and chacha, fresh herbs, and other regional products. These informal stalls are one of the more direct ways to engage with the produce of rural Adjara, and the honey in particular — from the wildflower meadows of the highland valleys — is worth trying. There's usually a small parking fee, and cafés operate in season.

The Queen Tamar Bridge

A short walk from the waterfall, the Queen Tamar Bridge crosses the Acharistskali River in a single elegant stone arch that has spanned this crossing for roughly eight centuries. It's one of the larger and best-known of Adjara's medieval arch bridges — about 20–30 meters long and some 6 meters high — built of local stone in a single arch whose proportions carry the structure's weight while keeping the graceful curve that defines Georgian medieval bridge design. The roadway is narrow, wide enough for foot traffic and pack animals, with low parapets that give unobstructed views of the river through the arch below. The bridge remains structurally sound and walkable, and crossing it gives a direct physical sense of the engineering achievement of building a span of this durability with only the tools and materials of the period.

About the name: the bridge is attributed to the reign of Queen Tamar, the monarch whose rule from 1184 to 1213 marked the peak of the Georgian Golden Age. It's worth being clear, though, that this is largely a popular attribution. Adjara alone has more than twenty medieval "Queen Tamar bridges," and the name attaches throughout Georgia to fine medieval construction regardless of whether Tamar's administration actually built each one — a reflection of how strongly her reign is associated with the country's golden age. What's certain is that the bridge is genuinely medieval: the construction technique, arch proportions, and stone-dressing are all consistent with the finest Georgian bridge-building of the 12th and 13th centuries, and local tradition holds it was made with a special lime-based mortar that hardened almost to stone, which would help explain its survival.

The setting adds a great deal. The Acharistskali flows through a wooded gorge here, the forest rising on both banks and the river audible from the bridge deck. The combination of medieval stone and mountain forest makes a composition that's genuinely beautiful rather than merely historically interesting — one of those monuments whose physical reality matches, and in some respects exceeds, the expectations its description sets. (A frank note: some visitors find the bridge's stonework looks relatively fresh, the result of restoration over the centuries — but the structure and setting are the draw.)

Nearby

Makhuntseti works well as the centerpiece of a mountain-Adjara half-day. The nearby Mirveti Waterfall (lower but with attractive cascades) and the Chorokhi–Acharistskali confluence are common additions, and the route is frequently sold as a "mountain Adjara" tour combining the waterfall and bridge with a Georgian wine-and-food tasting and folklore — the wider valley is also the heart of Adjara's wine and hospitality traditions.

Practical information

  • Location: Makhuntseti village, Adjara, about 30–35 km from Batumi along the Acharistskali (Batumi–Keda) valley road.
  • Getting there: By car or taxi from Batumi, about 40 minutes; included in many organized day tours. Hourly shuttle buses run from the Old Batumi bus station (ask to be dropped at Makhuntseti village); independent public transport is otherwise limited.
  • Hours/Admission: Outdoor and freely accessible at all times; a small parking fee applies (around 2 GEL).
  • Best time: Spring through autumn — the waterfall is most powerful in spring (snowmelt), and autumn offers the best forest color and most comfortable temperatures. Accessible year-round.
  • Bring: Comfortable shoes; it's cooler in the mountains than on the coast, even in summer.

faq.title: What is there to see at Makhuntseti?
faq.content: Two sights a short walk apart in the Adjarian highlands, about 30–35 km from Batumi: the Makhuntseti Waterfall — around 30 meters high, one of the tallest in Adjara, with a pool you can swim in during summer — and the Queen Tamar Bridge, a single-arch medieval stone bridge (12th–13th century) over the Acharistskali River. There's usually a small market of local vendors near the parking area selling honey, churchkhela, and homemade wine. It's one of the most popular half-day trips from Batumi.

faq.title: How do you get to Makhuntseti from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 40 minutes by car or taxi from Batumi, heading into the mountains along the Acharistskali (Batumi–Keda) valley road. It's included in many organized day tours, and hourly shuttle buses run from the Old Batumi bus station (tell the driver you want Makhuntseti village). Independent public transport is otherwise limited, so a car, taxi, or tour is easiest.

faq.title: Did Queen Tamar really build the bridge?
faq.content: Probably not this specific one. The bridge is genuinely medieval — its construction is consistent with the finest Georgian bridge-building of the 12th–13th centuries — but the "Queen Tamar" name is largely a popular attribution. Adjara alone has more than twenty such "Queen Tamar bridges," and her name attaches across Georgia to fine medieval construction because her reign (1184–1213) is so strongly linked with the country's golden age. So it dates to roughly her era, but the direct attribution is tradition rather than documented fact.

faq.title: Can you swim at the Makhuntseti Waterfall?
faq.content: Yes — the pool at the base is popular for swimming in summer (July and August), when the water is warm enough, and some visitors stand under the falling water itself. The spray keeps the air cool even on hot days. The waterfall is most powerful in spring from snowmelt, when swimming is colder and the flow stronger. As always with natural pools, take care with the current and slippery rocks.

faq.title: Is Makhuntseti worth visiting?
faq.content: For a half-day from Batumi, yes — it combines an easily reached, genuinely tall waterfall with an atmospheric medieval bridge in a forested gorge, plus a local market and (in season) swimming. It's very popular, so it can be busy in peak summer. Some visitors note the bridge's stonework looks relatively fresh from restoration, and the waterfall, while tall, is one stop rather than a full day — but as an easy, scenic introduction to mountain Adjara it's hard to beat.

machakhelaNationalPark.heroTitle: Machakhela National Park: Adjara's Forest Valley of the Gunsmiths
machakhelaNationalPark.content:
Machakhela National Park occupies the valley of the Machakhela River in the southwestern corner of Adjara, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus, running toward the Turkish border through a landscape of dense Colchic forest, mountain ridges, and small traditional villages that have kept a way of life shaped by the valley's particular mix of isolation and cultural pride. It's one of the least-visited protected areas in Georgia — considerably less developed for tourism than Mtirala to the north or the Borjomi-Kharagauli forests further inland — and that obscurity is the defining characteristic of what it offers. This is a place to explore without a fixed program, where the reward comes from the valley itself rather than from a set of designated attractions.

What is Machakhela National Park?

Machakhela National Park is a little-visited national park in southwestern Adjara, Georgia, established in 2012 and covering about 8,733 hectares of the Machakhela River valley toward the Turkish border. It protects dense Colchic (Colchian) forest — part of the 2021 UNESCO-listed Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands — alongside inhabited highland villages with a famous heritage of gunsmithing: the valley was renowned across the Caucasus for its "Machakhela" flintlock rifles. It's about an hour from Batumi, with hiking trails, waterfalls, medieval arched bridges, an ethnographic museum, and a strong cultural identity. There are no entrance gates, and access is free.

The forest and the valley

The park was established in 2012 and covers roughly 8,733 hectares of the Machakhela valley system, protecting the forested slopes and the river corridor from the lower valley floor up to the ridgelines approaching the Turkish border. The forest is the same Colchic subtropical type found in Mtirala — oriental beech, chestnut, hornbeam, and alder in the canopy, with relict and rare species including Colchic boxwood, Colchic hazelnut, Georgian walnut, rhododendron, and English yew, the whole system sustained by the high rainfall of the Adjarian highlands. As part of the Colchic forest ecosystem, the park lies within the area recognized in 2021 as the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands." But the valley configuration of Machakhela differs from the more enclosed gorge character of Mtirala: a wider valley floor with inhabited villages gives the landscape a more open and human quality.

The valley of the gunsmiths

What distinguishes Machakhela from a purely natural destination is the cultural layer embedded in the valley's history. The Machakhela valley was historically known throughout the Caucasus as a center of weapons production — specifically the manufacture of flintlock rifles of a design recognized for its quality. The "Machakhela" rifle was prized from the 18th century onward for its range and accuracy, cost more than comparable weapons of its era, and was sought after well beyond the region — traded to Turkey, Russia, and reportedly as far as Germany and France. The gunsmith tradition developed in the isolated mountain valley in response to the security needs of communities living in the borderland between Georgian, Ottoman, and later Russian spheres of influence, where the ability to produce and maintain weapons had direct practical significance. The craft passed down through families across generations and became both a technical tradition and an economic identity for the valley.

This history hasn't entirely disappeared. The broader cultural identity of the Machakhela communities retains a consciousness of this heritage that sets them apart from other Adjarian highland villages — the valley's single through-road is even known as the "Machakhela Gun Road," and a Gun Monument (built in 2007) stands as a symbol of the tradition. The Machakhela Historical-Ethnographic Museum, in the village of Chkhutuneti, documents the weapons-making craft — including the actual workbench and the names of the gun's makers — alongside the broader ethnographic history of the area. The valley also carries a distinctive religious history: Machakhela resisted Ottoman-era Islamization more strongly than most of Adjara, and it's often noted as the one part of the region without a functioning mosque.

The villages and daily life

The inhabited villages are as much a part of what Machakhela offers as the forest and the river. The traditional wooden houses of the Adjarian highland style — stone foundations, wooden upper stories, roofs steeply pitched against the heavy rainfall — appear throughout the valley in various states of preservation, from well-kept family homes to structures gradually being reclaimed by the vegetation. Agricultural life continues in its traditional patterns: cattle grazing the valley floor and lower slopes, small cultivation plots, the forest used for timber and forage as it has been for centuries. The valley is also known for its honey. The valley also holds medieval stone arched bridges (the "Tamar" bridges), and the ruins of old fortresses, scattered among the villages.

Interaction with valley residents tends toward the informal directness of rural Georgian hospitality — an invitation for coffee or wine is not unlikely if you stop near a village, and the absence of the tourist infrastructure that has modified this quality of encounter in more visited parts of Adjara means meetings with local people here retain a genuineness that organized cultural tourism cannot replicate.

Exploring the valley

The most rewarding approach to Machakhela is an unhurried drive through the valley, stopping at points of interest along the road and taking short walks into the forest or down to the river where the terrain allows. The road through the valley is generally manageable for a standard vehicle, though some sections are unpaved and conditions after heavy rain can make the surface more challenging; a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance gives more flexibility for the smaller side valleys and higher sections. The park also has marked walking trails (such as the roughly four-kilometer Archangel Trail) that integrate with the road route.

Short hikes from the road into the forest reveal the full character of the Colchic vegetation — the density of the understory, the age of the trees, the moss-covered quality of every surface in the high humidity — far more directly than a valley-floor drive. The river, running clear and cold through the valley bottom, is pleasant to follow on foot where paths allow, and the swimming holes and riverside spots along the lower valley are used by local families in summer.

Practical information

  • Location: Machakhela valley, Khelvachauri Municipality, Adjara, southwest of Batumi toward the Turkish border. The administration/visitor center is in the valley.
  • Getting there: About an hour (roughly 30 km) from Batumi by private car or taxi; no regular public transport serves the valley. The road heads south/southwest from Batumi along the Chorokhi River before entering the Machakhela valley — straightforward, but check local advice on road conditions after heavy rain.
  • Hours/Admission: The park is open at all times, with no entrance gates at the boundary, and entry is free. The museum keeps its own hours.
  • Best time: May through October. Spring brings the greenest vegetation and highest river levels; autumn brings color to the deciduous trees among the evergreen canopy.

faq.title: What is Machakhela National Park?
faq.content: Machakhela National Park is a little-visited national park in southwestern Adjara, Georgia, established in 2012 and covering about 8,733 hectares of the Machakhela River valley toward the Turkish border. It protects dense Colchic forest — part of the 2021 UNESCO-listed Colchic Rainforests and Wetlands — together with inhabited highland villages famous for a heritage of gunsmithing. It offers hiking, waterfalls, medieval bridges, an ethnographic museum, and a strong local cultural identity, about an hour from Batumi.

faq.title: Why is Machakhela known for guns?
faq.content: The valley was renowned across the Caucasus from the 18th century for the "Machakhela" flintlock rifle, prized for its range and accuracy and traded as far as Turkey, Russia, and reportedly Germany and France. The gunsmith tradition grew up in this remote borderland valley where producing and maintaining weapons had real practical importance, and it became an economic and cultural identity passed down through families. Today a Gun Monument and the valley's ethnographic museum commemorate the craft, and the main road is even called the "Machakhela Gun Road."

faq.title: Is Machakhela worth visiting, and what is there to do?
faq.content: For travelers who want a quiet, authentic corner of Adjara, yes. It's not a polished attraction with a fixed program — the appeal is the valley itself: driving the gun road, short forest hikes (like the Archangel Trail), the clear river and summer swimming spots, the medieval arched bridges, the gunsmith museum, and the traditional villages. It rewards unhurried, independent exploration more than ticking off sights, and it's far less developed than Mtirala or the more famous parks.

faq.title: How do you get to Machakhela National Park from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about an hour (roughly 30 km) from Batumi by private car or taxi, heading south/southwest along the Chorokhi River before turning up the Machakhela valley. There's no regular public transport into the valley. Some sections of the valley road are unpaved and can be rough after heavy rain, so a vehicle with reasonable ground clearance helps, and it's worth checking road conditions locally before setting out.

faq.title: How is Machakhela different from Mtirala National Park?
faq.content: Both protect the same lush Colchic forest, but they feel different. Mtirala is a more enclosed, gorge-like rainforest park focused on the natural environment (it's one of the wettest places in Georgia). Machakhela is a wider, more open inhabited valley where the villages, the gunsmith heritage, the bridges, and rural life are as much the draw as the forest — and it's even less visited. If you want nature plus living culture and near-solitude, Machakhela; for the classic rainforest experience closer to Batumi, Mtirala.

gonioFortress.heroTitle: Gonio Fortress (Apsaros): Georgia's Roman Stronghold near Batumi
gonioFortress.content:
Gonio Fortress stands near the mouth of the Chorokhi River, about 15 kilometers south of Batumi in Adjara, on the coastal road toward the Turkish border, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus. Its rectangular stone walls rise from the flat coastal terrain in a way that immediately communicates the seriousness of its original military function. One of the oldest and best-preserved ancient fortifications in Georgia, it holds Roman, Byzantine, Ottoman, and Georgian layers accumulated across two millennia — which makes it one of the more historically substantial sites in Adjara, despite its modest scale beside the dramatic medieval fortresses found elsewhere in the country.

What is Gonio Fortress?

Gonio Fortress (known in antiquity as Apsaros) is a 1st-century AD Roman fort on Georgia's Black Sea coast, about 15 km south of Batumi near the Turkish border. Its rectangular walls (roughly 222 × 195 m, with 18 surviving towers of an original ~22) enclose an open archaeological site and a small museum, with Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman layers. It's the oldest fortress in Georgia, tied by legend to Jason and the Argonauts, and by Orthodox tradition to the tomb of the Apostle Matthias. It's an easy half-day trip from Batumi, combined with the nearby beaches.

Roman origins: the fortress of Apsaros

Gonio was known in antiquity as Apsaros (Apsaros/Apsaruntos), and its origins as a significant military installation date to the Roman period, when it served as one of the easternmost garrison posts of the Roman Empire on the Black Sea coast. It's mentioned by Pliny the Elder in the 1st century AD, and by the 2nd century it was a well-fortified Roman town within Colchis, known to have had a theater and a hippodrome. The Romans recognized the strategic value of the location — at the mouth of the Chorokhi, near the edge of the empire's sphere of influence, on the route linking the Black Sea to the Caucasian interior — and built a fortress here of considerable scale and sophistication for its time. The walls, which stand to an impressive height along much of their circuit, preserve substantial sections of Roman construction alongside later Byzantine and Ottoman rebuilding, and the different periods are legible in the masonry to those who know how to read them. The site came under Byzantine influence, saw a short-lived Genoese trading presence, and was taken by the Ottomans in 1547; they held it until 1878, when Adjara passed to the Russian Empire.

The Argonaut legend

Gonio's ancient name ties it to one of the oldest stories associated with this coast. By legend, the name Apsaros derives from Apsyrtus (Apsyrtos), son of Aeëtes, the king of Colchis — the same king whose daughter Medea helped Jason and the Argonauts win the Golden Fleece. In the myth, Apsyrtus was killed during the Argonauts' flight and is said to have been buried at this site. It's legend rather than history, but it connects Gonio to the wider Colchis–Golden Fleece mythology of western Georgia — the same story commemorated by the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square and referenced by the Argo Cable Car.

The apostolic tradition: the tomb of St. Matthias

Gonio carries a religious significance that extends well beyond military history. According to Georgian Orthodox tradition, the fortress contains the tomb of Saint Matthias — the apostle chosen by lot to replace Judas Iscariot among the twelve — who is said to have traveled to the eastern Black Sea coast as part of the early Christian missionary expansion and to have died and been buried at Apsaros. The tradition is long-standing and the site receives pilgrims as well as historians. It's important to be clear, though, that the tomb is symbolic and unverified: it is marked within the fortress (large cross-stone pedestals stand at the spot, in the area of the Roman praetorium, the garrison commander's residence), but its location has not been demonstrated archaeologically, and the Georgian authorities currently prohibit excavation near the presumed gravesite. It's best understood as cultural and religious memory rather than a confirmed archaeological find.

The fortress

The outer walls form a rectangle of roughly 222 by 195 meters (a perimeter of about 900 meters, enclosing some 4.5 hectares), with towers at intervals along the curtain wall and at the corners — a layout characteristic of Roman military architecture that gave overlapping fields of fire from the towers and minimized blind spots in the defensive coverage. Eighteen towers survive today of an original twenty-two. The walls stand several meters high along most of their circuit, and walking the perimeter gives a clear sense of the scale and organization of the original garrison. The masonry quality varies — reflecting the different periods and resources across twenty centuries of construction and repair — but the overall impression is of a fortification built to last, which it largely has.

The interior is an open archaeological zone where excavations (ongoing since the 1990s) have uncovered evidence of occupation across multiple periods — Roman-era structures, Byzantine-period buildings, and later phases, in various states of excavation and preservation. A small on-site museum presents the most significant finds: pottery, coins, military equipment, and architectural fragments documenting the life of the garrison and the trading community that grew up around it. The grounds also feature ornamental planting, including floral designs of the Georgian and Adjaran flags.

The setting is worth noting. The flat coastal terrain, with the Chorokhi delta nearby and the Black Sea accessible from the walls, gives the site an openness and a connection to the natural geography that explains the Roman decision to fortify exactly here — it guarded the entrances to the Chorokhi and Acharistskali valleys, linking the interior of southwestern Georgia to the coast. The mountains of the Adjarian interior rise inland, and on clear days the Turkish coast is visible across the water to the south — a view the Roman commanders used to monitor coastal traffic much as a modern visitor uses it to get their bearings.

Getting there

Gonio is about 15 to 20 minutes from Batumi by car along the coastal road south toward the Turkish border. Marshrutkas and city buses running between Batumi and Sarpi pass through Gonio and stop near the fortress, so public-transport access is straightforward. The site is commonly included in organized tours of the Adjara coast and is easily combined with the Gonio and Kvariati beaches along the coastal road, or with the Botanical Garden north of Batumi for a fuller day out.

Practical information

  • Location: Gonio village, Adjara, about 15 km south of Batumi on the coastal road (about 4 km north of the Sarpi/Turkish border).
  • Hours: Daily, roughly 10:00–18:00 (may vary seasonally).
  • Admission: Paid entry at the gate; the on-site museum is included. As a working archaeological museum-reserve, hours and access can change.
  • Best to know: Some wall sections can be climbed for the view; bring sun protection (the site is open and exposed). Easily paired with the nearby beaches.

faq.title: What is Gonio Fortress?
faq.content: Gonio Fortress, known in antiquity as Apsaros, is a 1st-century AD Roman fort on Georgia's Black Sea coast, about 15 km south of Batumi near the Turkish border. Considered the oldest fortress in Georgia, its rectangular walls (about 222 × 195 m, with 18 surviving towers) enclose an open archaeological site and a small museum, with layers from the Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman periods. It's tied by legend to Jason and the Argonauts and by Orthodox tradition to the tomb of the Apostle Matthias.

faq.title: Is the tomb of St. Matthias really at Gonio?
faq.content: There's a long-standing Georgian Orthodox tradition that the Apostle Matthias — chosen to replace Judas among the twelve — preached and was buried here, and a symbolic tomb is marked within the fortress with cross-stone pedestals. But it's unverified: the location has never been confirmed archaeologically, and excavation near the presumed gravesite is currently prohibited. It's best understood as religious and cultural tradition rather than an established archaeological fact.

faq.title: Why is Gonio connected to the Argonauts?
faq.content: The fortress's ancient name, Apsaros, is linked by legend to Apsyrtus, son of Aeëtes, the king of Colchis (western Georgia) — the same king whose daughter Medea helped Jason win the Golden Fleece. In the myth, Apsyrtus was killed during the Argonauts' escape and supposedly buried at this site. It's legend, not history, but it ties Gonio to the Colchis–Golden Fleece mythology also celebrated by the Medea statue in Batumi's Europe Square.

faq.title: How do you get to Gonio Fortress from Batumi?
faq.content: It's about 15–20 minutes by car along the coastal road south toward the Turkish border. Marshrutkas and city buses between Batumi and Sarpi pass through Gonio and stop near the fortress, so public transport is easy, and taxis serve the route. It's commonly part of organized Adjara-coast tours and pairs well with the nearby Gonio and Kvariati beaches.

faq.title: Is Gonio Fortress worth visiting?
faq.content: For travelers interested in ancient history it's genuinely worthwhile — it's the oldest fortress in Georgia, with two millennia of Roman, Byzantine, and Ottoman layers, a small museum of finds, walls you can walk, and the Argonaut and apostolic legends attached. Expectations should be calibrated, though: it's mainly walls and an excavation zone rather than a dramatic medieval castle, so it rewards historical curiosity more than spectacle. The nearby beaches make it an easy, pleasant half-day from Batumi.

goderdziPass.heroTitle: Goderdzi Pass: The Mountain Crossing Between Adjara and Samtskhe-Javakheti
goderdziPass.content:
The Goderdzi Pass crosses the watershed between Adjara and Samtskhe-Javakheti at an elevation of around 2,025 meters, in the country of Georgia in the South Caucasus, connecting the subtropical coastal zone of the Black Sea with the high volcanic plateau of southern Georgia in a single road journey of remarkable geographical contrast. It's not yet widely known on the international tourist circuit, and that relative obscurity is part of what makes it worth including in an itinerary — it gives access to genuinely remote mountain terrain and a landscape transition of unusual drama, without the infrastructure and crowds that have grown up around Georgia's more celebrated mountain routes.

What is the Goderdzi Pass?

The Goderdzi Pass is a high mountain pass (around 2,025 m) on the Batumi–Akhaltsikhe road in southwestern Georgia, crossing the Arsiani ridge between the Adjara region and Samtskhe-Javakheti. It links the lush, subtropical Adjarian coast — via the highland towns of Khulo and Shuakhevi — with the open volcanic plateau of southern Georgia and the road toward Akhaltsikhe, Rabati Castle, and Vardzia. It's known for a dramatic landscape transition, a small but growing ski resort, and a rough, partly unpaved road that needs a high-clearance or 4x4 vehicle. It works best as a scenic crossing linking an Adjara trip to southern Georgia.

The journey up

The drive from Batumi to the pass summit is roughly 100–110 kilometers (the rough pass road itself, between Khulo and Akhaltsikhe, runs about 80 km), and it takes considerably longer than the distance suggests, because the road climbs through a succession of environments that reward slow progress over efficient transit. The lower section follows the Adjarian valleys through the dense subtropical vegetation of the coastal slopes — the same lush forest character seen in Mtirala National Park — passing tea plantations and small agricultural villages in the enclosed green world of the lower Adjara highlands. As the elevation rises the vegetation changes progressively: subtropical species give way to temperate forest, then to the scrub and open meadow of the subalpine zone, until the road emerges above the treeline onto the open plateau landscape of the pass itself.

The landscape at the pass

The terrain around the summit is defined by the open, rolling character of the high volcanic plateau that runs through this part of southern Georgia — wide meadows, low ridgelines, and the particular quality of light and air that high-altitude plateau environments produce. (The pass area is geologically notable: its volcanic formations preserve fossil plant remains, the so-called "Goderdzi flora.") The contrast with the enclosed forest valleys of the Adjarian slopes below is complete, and it arrives with some suddenness as the road clears the treeline; the sense of spatial release — from the narrow green tunnel of the forested approach to the open sky and distant horizons of the plateau — is one of the more memorable transitions on any road journey in Georgia. At the summit itself there's little more than a few small shops, a hotel or two, and a distinctive Soviet-era bus stop, with the view back down into Adjara as the main reward.

The weather is genuinely mountain weather — changeable and often dramatic. Mist and cloud move through quickly, and a clear morning can give way to complete cloud cover within an hour before clearing again. This variability is part of the experience rather than a limitation; the pass in mist, the road visible only a short way ahead and the surrounding landscape appearing and disappearing in the cloud, has its own atmospheric quality. Snow can occur at the summit elevation at almost any time of year, and the road may be closed or difficult in winter outside the organized ski operation. Near the pass, a short paved road leads to the small Green Lake, and the Beshumi area is a traditional Adjarian summer-settlement and highland resort worth knowing about.

Goderdzi Ski Resort

A ski resort has been developed in the terrain around the pass — first built between roughly 2012 and 2015 — making Goderdzi one of the higher-altitude winter-sports destinations in Georgia. It's less developed and considerably less crowded than Gudauri, and the infrastructure is still expanding: the lift system and on-mountain facilities are more modest than at the established resorts, and accommodation in the immediate area is limited (a handful of hotels and cottages). For skiers seeking uncrowded slopes and a more remote experience than the main Georgian resorts offer — and freeriders after off-piste powder — those characteristics are assets rather than limitations. Its proximity to the Black Sea brings heavy, high-quality snow, and the season typically runs from December into March or April, with January and February usually the best for snow.

The route as an itinerary

The Goderdzi Pass works most naturally as a crossing point rather than a destination in isolation. From the pass the road continues into the Samtskhe-Javakheti highlands, eventually connecting with the Akhaltsikhe road and the broader southern-Georgia circuit that includes Rabati Castle, Khertvisi Fortress, and the Vardzia cave monastery. Treating the pass as the link between an Adjara-based coastal itinerary and a southern-Georgia historical one gives the journey a logical purpose that heightens the landscape transition — arriving in Samtskhe-Javakheti through the "back door" of the Goderdzi highlands, after the lush subtropical forests of Adjara, creates a more vivid impression of Georgia's geographical diversity than any more conventional approach.

The road surface on the pass section can be rough — particularly the unpaved stretch between Khulo and Zarzma — and the condition varies with season and recent maintenance (road-improvement works have been underway). A vehicle with high clearance, and ideally four-wheel drive, is strongly advisable; in an ordinary car the crossing is slow and uncomfortable at best. Checking road conditions locally before attempting the crossing in early spring or late autumn, when snow and mud can make the surface unpredictable, is strongly recommended.

Practical information

  • Location: Between the Adjara and Samtskhe-Javakheti regions, on the Arsiani ridge; summit around 2,025 meters. On the Batumi–Akhaltsikhe (Trans-Adjara) road, via Khulo and Shuakhevi.
  • Distance: Roughly 100–110 km from Batumi to the pass; the rough Khulo–Akhaltsikhe pass road is about 80 km.
  • Getting there: By private car (high-clearance / 4x4 recommended) or organized tour from Batumi; allow around three hours or more from Batumi depending on conditions and stops. Buses run Batumi–Khulo, but the final leg to the pass/resort needs a hired high-clearance vehicle; no regular public transport serves the pass road itself.
  • Best time: Late May through October for the drive and hiking; December through March (into April) for the ski resort, weather permitting.
  • Road conditions: Rough and weather-dependent, partly unpaved; check locally before traveling, especially in spring and autumn.

faq.title: What is the Goderdzi Pass?
faq.content: The Goderdzi Pass is a high mountain pass (around 2,025 m) on the Batumi–Akhaltsikhe road in southwestern Georgia, crossing the Arsiani ridge between the Adjara region and Samtskhe-Javakheti. It links the subtropical Adjarian coast — via the highland towns of Khulo and Shuakhevi — with the open volcanic plateau of southern Georgia and the route toward Akhaltsikhe and Vardzia. It's known for its dramatic landscape transition, a growing ski resort, and a rough, partly unpaved road.

faq.title: Do you need a 4x4 to drive the Goderdzi Pass?
faq.content: For the pass section it's strongly recommended. Parts of the road — especially the unpaved stretch between Khulo and Zarzma — are rough, and a vehicle with high clearance and ideally four-wheel drive makes the crossing far easier; in an ordinary car it's slow and uncomfortable. Conditions vary with season and ongoing roadworks, and the pass can be impassable in winter outside the ski operation, so check locally before setting out, particularly in spring and autumn.

faq.title: Where does the Goderdzi Pass road go?
faq.content: It runs between Adjara and Samtskhe-Javakheti on the Batumi–Akhaltsikhe (Trans-Adjara) road. From the Adjarian side it climbs from the coast through Keda, Shuakhevi, and Khulo; on the southern side it descends toward Zarzma, Adigeni, and Akhaltsikhe, connecting to the southern-Georgia circuit of Rabati Castle, Khertvisi Fortress, and the Vardzia cave monastery. That makes it a natural link between an Adjara coastal trip and a southern-Georgia history route.

faq.title: Is there skiing at Goderdzi?
faq.content: Yes — Goderdzi Ski Resort, developed around 2012–2015 on the pass, is one of Georgia's higher and newer winter-sports areas. It's smaller and far less crowded than Gudauri, with more modest lifts and limited local accommodation, but its closeness to the Black Sea brings heavy, high-quality snow, and it's popular with freeriders seeking uncrowded off-piste powder. The season runs roughly December into March or April, with January–February best.

faq.title: How do you get to the Goderdzi Pass from Batumi?
faq.content: By private car (high-clearance or 4x4 recommended) or organized tour — it's roughly 100–110 km and around three hours or more from Batumi depending on conditions and stops. Buses run from Batumi to Khulo, but the final leg up to the pass or resort requires hiring a local high-clearance vehicle; there's no regular public transport on the pass road itself.

thingsToDoBorjomi.heroTitle: Things to Do in Borjomi, Georgia: Springs, Sulfur Pools, and Forest
thingsToDoBorjomi.content:
Borjomi is one of Georgia's best-known resort towns — Georgia the country in the South Caucasus — set in a green valley ringed by forested hills. Unlike the bigger cities, it isn't about architecture or urban life; it's about nature, fresh air, and slowing down. The town has been famous for its mineral water since the 19th century, when it became a favorite of the Russian imperial family, and it still carries that identity as a place to walk, soak, and recover between more active parts of a trip. It works especially well as a stop between Tbilisi and Akhaltsikhe, or as a short nature escape from the capital.

What is there to do in Borjomi?

The main things to do in Borjomi are walking Central Park and tasting the mineral water at the source, soaking in the forest sulfur pools beyond the park, riding the cable car for a view over the valley, and hiking in Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park. Add the Romanov palace grounds at Likani and easy onward trips to Akhaltsikhe, Vardzia, and Bakuriani, and Borjomi fills a relaxed full day or works as a base for the wider region. This is a destination for nature and recovery rather than monuments and sightseeing.

Walk through Borjomi Central Park

Borjomi Central Park is the main gathering place in town and the starting point for most visits. It follows the Borjomula River and combines walking paths, small bridges, forested stretches, and open spaces, with a relaxed feel where locals and visitors come to walk and sit by the water. The highlight is the mineral water spring, where you can taste Borjomi water straight from the source — the flavor is strong and a little unusual, but that's part of it. You can easily spend a few hours here without a plan, walking deeper into the park or sitting by the river.

Taste the mineral water at the source

Borjomi's identity is tied to its mineral water, one of the country's most recognizable products and exported internationally. Tasting it at the source is different from the bottled version: warm and strongly mineral rather than the cold, carbonated drink most people know. Whether or not you like the taste, it's central to understanding Borjomi's history and reputation as a health resort. One honest note — many first-timers find the warm source water an acquired taste, so don't be surprised if you prefer the bottled kind.

Visit the Borjomi sulfur pools

The Borjomi sulfur pools are one of the most enjoyable things to do beyond Central Park. You reach them on a marked path that runs deeper into the forest, and the walk along the river through quiet, green surroundings is part of the experience. Tourism information places them roughly 2 kilometers beyond the rear of Borjomi Park, which makes them an easy extension of a park visit.

The pools are open-air and fed by warm sulfur water. According to the Georgian National Tourism Administration, the water generally sits around 32–38°C, which makes the pools appealing in any season, including the colder months — the contrast is especially memorable in autumn and winter, when the forest air is cool and the water stays warm. This isn't a luxury spa; the appeal is natural and local, closer to a forest bath than a formal wellness center. The site has showers and changing rooms, which makes a visit practical. For anyone spending a full day in Borjomi, the pools give the day a proper finishing point and round out the town's identity around bathing and recovery, not just drinking the water.

Take the cable car for a view over Borjomi

From Central Park, a cable car runs up to a viewpoint above the town. The ride is short, but it gives a good sense of Borjomi's layout — a narrow valley wrapped in dense forest — and from the top you can see how the town blends into the landscape. A simple activity, but worth doing to understand the setting.

Explore Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park

Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park is one of the largest protected areas in Georgia and a major reason to visit the region. It offers forest trails, hiking routes of different lengths, and large stretches of undisturbed nature with wildlife. You don't need to be an experienced hiker to enjoy it — even short walks near the park entrances give a good impression of the environment, while longer routes deeper in need proper planning and equipment. For travelers who like nature, this is one of the more rewarding areas in Georgia.

Visit the Romanov palace grounds at Likani

Just outside Borjomi, the Romanov palace at Likani reflects the town's past as a resort for the Russian imperial family. The palace is now a state residence and its interior is not generally open to visitors, but the surrounding area can be explored and gives a sense of how Borjomi developed as a 19th-century health destination. Check access before making a special trip, as it varies.

Relax in the forest and by the river

One of the main reasons to come to Borjomi is simply to slow down. The combination of forest, river, and clean air makes a calm environment that contrasts with the busier cities. Walking without a plan, sitting by the river, or just spending time in nature is a real part of the visit — and a useful reset if you've come from a hectic stretch of travel.

Combine Borjomi with nearby destinations

Borjomi is well placed for combining with other destinations: Akhaltsikhe and its Rabati Castle, the Vardzia cave monastery beyond it, and the mountain resort of Bakuriani. That makes it a flexible stop within a larger southern Georgia itinerary, and many travelers base in Borjomi to reach the others.

Why Borjomi is worth visiting

Borjomi offers something different from the rest of Georgia. It isn't about monuments or busy sightseeing — it's about nature and fresh air, relaxation, light exploration, and a historical spa culture. It works as a balance within a trip, especially after more active or urban destinations. The trade-off is exactly that: travelers chasing big-ticket sights will find more elsewhere, while those who want to slow down get the most from it.

faq.title: What are the main things to do in Borjomi?
faq.content: Walk Central Park and taste the mineral water at the source, soak in the forest sulfur pools beyond the park, ride the cable car for a view over the valley, and hike in Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park. The Romanov palace grounds at Likani and trips to Akhaltsikhe, Vardzia, and Bakuriani round it out.

faq.title: Are the Borjomi sulfur pools worth visiting?
faq.content: Yes, especially as a full-day finish. The open-air pools are about 2 km beyond the rear of Central Park along a forest path, fed by warm sulfur water at around 32–38°C, with showers and changing rooms. It's a natural, local setting rather than a luxury spa, and especially nice in autumn and winter.

faq.title: Can you drink Borjomi mineral water at the source?
faq.content: Yes, in Central Park. The source water is warm and strongly mineral, different from the cold, carbonated bottled version most people know. The taste divides opinion, but it's central to the town's identity as a health resort.

faq.title: Do you need to be an experienced hiker for Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park?
faq.content: No. Short walks near the park entrances give a good impression of the forest without much effort. Longer routes deeper in the park require proper planning and equipment.

faq.title: What can you combine with Borjomi?
faq.content: Akhaltsikhe and Rabati Castle, the Vardzia cave monastery, and the ski resort of Bakuriani are all nearby, which makes Borjomi a flexible base for a southern Georgia itinerary.

borjomi.heroTitle: Borjomi, Georgia: Mineral Water, the National Park, and a Spa-Town History
borjomi.content:
Borjomi is a small resort town in the Samtskhe-Javakheti region of southern Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — at around 800 meters in the narrow gorge of the Mtkvari River. About 11,000 people live here, but the name carries far beyond the town: across the former Soviet Union, Borjomi means the mineral water bottled here since the 19th century and exported for well over a hundred years. That association is fair, but it's only part of the story. Borjomi is also the gateway to one of the largest protected forests in the Caucasus, a town with a distinctive imperial-era character, and a practical base for some of southern Georgia's most remarkable historical sites.

The setting is the first thing you notice. The Mtkvari gorge is deep and densely forested, with slopes rising sharply on both sides and the town occupying a thin strip of valley floor. Conifer and mixed deciduous forest covers nearly everything above the rooftops. The air is noticeably cleaner and cooler than in Tbilisi, about 160 kilometers to the northeast, and the mix of altitude, forest, and river valley makes a microclimate that's pleasant for much of the year.

Why visit Borjomi?

The main reasons to visit Borjomi are the mineral springs in its Central Park, the hiking in Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, the cool forested climate, and the town's 19th-century spa-town architecture. It also works as the hub for a tight regional loop with Bakuriani, Akhaltsikhe, and Vardzia. It suits travelers who want nature and a slower pace, families escaping summer heat, and anyone combining it with the nearby ski resort. One honest note up front: drinking the warm spring water straight is more curiosity than pleasure — the bottled version most people know is cold and carbonated, which is a very different experience.

The mineral springs and Central Park

Borjomi's Central Park is the heart of its resort identity, on the valley floor along the Borjomula River where it meets the Mtkvari. Laid out in the imperial period and expanded since, it has riverside walkways, bridges, gardens, and a small funicular climbing the forested hillside above. It's a genuinely pleasant public space, green and well maintained, and busy with locals and visitors on warm days.

The mineral spring itself is in the park, where water flows from a tap into a small pool at a natural temperature of around 38°C. You can drink from the source free of charge, and many people do — but the taste divides opinion sharply: strongly mineralized, warm, with a pronounced sulfurous quality. The commercially bottled version is cold and carbonated, which makes it far more approachable; the spring straight from the tap is something to try once rather than enjoy. The water's chemistry — sodium bicarbonate and trace minerals of volcanic origin — has been the basis of therapeutic claims since the 19th century, mainly around digestion. The funicular at the top of the park links to forest paths on the hillside, an easy way into the terrain surrounding the town.

Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park

The most significant natural asset here is Borjomi-Kharagauli National Park, which effectively begins at the edge of town and extends west across a vast area of mountain forest. Established in its current form in 2001, it covers about 85,000 hectares and protects one of the largest contiguous temperate forests in the Caucasus. It's often described as one of the larger national parks in Europe, though that comparison depends on how boundaries are counted.

The terrain runs from forested valleys around 800 meters near Borjomi to alpine meadows and rocky peaks above 2,600 meters. Forests are mostly mixed deciduous and conifer — beech, oak, hornbeam, and fir at different elevations — and the park supports brown bear, wolf, lynx, chamois, and red deer, plus many bird species, though sightings take patience and luck.

Marked trails of varying length start from the visitor center near Borjomi. Shorter routes of roughly two to six hours suit day visitors, passing through representative forest, crossing streams, and opening to views over the ridges. Longer multi-day routes cross the park east to west, finishing at the town of Kharagauli, and need advance planning, proper equipment, and some mountain experience. Guided horseback riding is also available. The visitor center provides trail maps, current conditions, and guides for those who'd rather not navigate alone — worth using, since the longer routes are remote.
