Sunset over the Black Sea is a highlight, and after dark the lit landmarks and the Ardagani Lake fountain show (summer evenings, often from around 9 pm) make it lively. Summer is peak season; spring and autumn are pleasant and quieter.

faq.title: Is there a beach on Batumi Boulevard?
faq.content: Yes — the beach runs parallel to much of the boulevard and is accessible at many points. Like the rest of the Georgian Black Sea coast, it's a shingle (pebble) beach rather than sand, with warm water and green mountains visible inland. Water shoes are worth bringing for comfort on the stones. The boulevard itself separates the beach from the city, so cafés and amenities are right behind the shore.

batumiCentralMosque.heroTitle: Batumi Central Mosque (Orta Jame): The City's Only Mosque
batumiCentralMosque.content:
The Batumi Central Mosque — known locally as Orta Jame, "Middle Mosque," a name recording its historical position between two other mosques that no longer exist — stands in the older part of the city in Batumi, Adjara, on the Black Sea coast of Georgia, the country in the South Caucasus. Its minaret and gilded dome rise above the surrounding rooftops as one of the more distinctive elements of the older Batumi skyline. It is the only functioning mosque in the city and the main place of worship for Batumi's Muslim community, which is drawn substantially from the Adjarian population whose conversion to Islam during the centuries of Ottoman rule gave this region its particular religious character within predominantly Orthodox Christian Georgia.

What is the Batumi Central Mosque?

The Batumi Central Mosque, locally called Orta Jame ("middle mosque"), is the only active mosque in Batumi and the religious heart of the city's Muslim community. Built in the 19th century during the Ottoman period (commonly dated to 1866) and commissioned by the prominent Muslim Georgian Khimshiashvili family, it's a late-Ottoman provincial-style building with a tall minaret, a gilded dome, and a richly painted interior. It reflects the distinctive Muslim heritage of Adjara within mostly Orthodox Christian Georgia. It's an active mosque, free to visit outside prayer times, with modest dress required.

Adjara's Muslim heritage

The mosque reflects the specific demographic history of Adjara. Unlike most of Georgia, where the overwhelming majority belongs to the Georgian Orthodox Church, Adjara has a significant Muslim community — descendants of Georgians who converted during the Ottoman period, from roughly the 16th century onward. When Russia annexed the region following the 1877–78 Russo-Turkish War, it incorporated a population that included a substantial Muslim community in parts of Adjara, and that community has maintained its religious identity through the Russian imperial period, the Soviet decades (when religious practice was officially suppressed but never entirely eliminated), and into the post-independence era, when freedom of worship was restored. Adjara today is religiously mixed, with both Muslim and Orthodox Christian communities, and the mosque stands as a visible expression of that heritage.

The building

The mosque was built in the 19th century during the Ottoman period — most sources date it to 1866, though some give 1886 — and it was commissioned by the family of the Khimshiashvili, prominent Muslim Georgian nobles of Adjara. (An earlier wooden mosque on or near the site, associated with the same family, is said to have burned down before the present building was raised.) It is a typical example of late-Ottoman provincial architecture: a two-story rectangular building with a tall, slender minaret and a large gilded dome that draw the eye from a distance. The exterior is relatively restrained — plastered and largely white — with the decorative ambition concentrated inside rather than expressed outwardly.

The name Orta Jame ("middle mosque") records that it once stood between two other Batumi mosques — Azizie Jame and Ahmed Pasha Jame — neither of which survives, leaving Orta Jame as the city's only mosque. During the Soviet era it was closed and repurposed (used for a time as a warehouse) before being restored to religious use in the mid-20th century, and it has functioned as an active mosque since.

The interior is the more rewarding part of the building for visitors, and it contrasts noticeably with the modest exterior. The prayer hall is decorated in the Ottoman and post-Ottoman Islamic tradition — painted surfaces in blue, gold, and other colors, geometric and floral patterns across the walls and ceiling, and calligraphic Quranic inscriptions worked into the decorative program around the dome and prayer niche. Original wooden patterns and ornaments survive on the balconies and railings, made in the tradition of Georgian wooden architecture, and the minbar — the pulpit from which the Friday sermon is delivered — is among the more finely worked individual elements. An upper gallery serves as the space where women pray. The overall effect is warm and richly colored, quite different from the more austere character of some Islamic prayer spaces.

Religious life and visiting

The mosque is an active religious institution in daily use, with the five daily prayers observed and Friday prayers drawing the largest congregation of the week — large enough that worshippers sometimes pray on the street outside at busy Friday prayers. Visitors are welcome outside prayer times, and the interior is most easily experienced in the mid-morning and mid-afternoon periods when the space is quiet. A visit is typically calm and unhurried, the building occupied by individuals at prayer or simply present, and the contrast between the busy commercial streets immediately outside and the quiet of the interior is one of the more immediate transitions available on a walk through central Batumi. The mosque also runs a small school where children study the Quran.

Its position in the older part of the city — within easy walking distance of Europe Square, Piazza, and the historic 19th-century commercial district — makes it a natural element of a walking route through central Batumi for visitors interested in the city's religious and architectural diversity. Its proximity to the Orthodox churches and other religious buildings of the center reflects the mixed religious character of Adjara in a way the boulevard and seafront developments, focused on contemporary leisure, do not.

Practical information

  • Location: Central Batumi, in the older part of the city, a short walk from Europe Square (commonly cited on Kutaisi Street).
  • Hours: Open daily for the five daily prayers; visitors welcome outside active prayer times. Friday midday prayers draw the largest congregation, and visitor access around that time may be limited.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Dress code: Modest clothing for all visitors; women should cover their heads (scarves are typically available at the entrance); shoes are removed before entering the prayer hall.

faq.title: What is the Batumi Central Mosque?
faq.content: It's the only active mosque in Batumi, locally known as Orta Jame ("middle mosque"). Built in the 19th century during the Ottoman period (commonly dated 1866) and commissioned by the Muslim Georgian Khimshiashvili family, it's a late-Ottoman provincial-style building with a tall minaret, a gilded dome, and a richly painted interior. It serves the Muslim community of Adjara and is a symbol of the region's religious diversity.

faq.title: Why does Batumi have a mosque when Georgia is mostly Orthodox Christian?
faq.content: Because Adjara has a distinct history. Under Ottoman rule from around the 16th century, much of the local Georgian population converted to Islam, and although the region was annexed by Russia after the 1877–78 Russo-Turkish War, the Muslim community endured through the imperial and Soviet periods. Adjara today is religiously mixed — both Muslim and Orthodox Christian — which sets it apart from most of Georgia, and the mosque reflects that heritage.

faq.title: Why is it called Orta Jame?
faq.content: "Orta Jame" means "middle mosque." It got the name because it once stood between two other Batumi mosques — Azizie Jame and Ahmed Pasha Jame — neither of which has survived. With those two gone, Orta Jame is now the only mosque left in the city, but the name preserves the memory of when it stood in the middle of three.

faq.title: Can tourists visit the Batumi mosque, and what should they wear?
faq.content: Yes — visitors are welcome outside active prayer times, and it's free. Modest dress is required: women should cover their heads (scarves are usually available at the entrance), and everyone removes their shoes before entering the prayer hall. The quietest times to visit are mid-morning and mid-afternoon; avoid Friday midday, when the main weekly prayers draw the largest congregation.

faq.title: What is there to see at the mosque?
faq.content: The contrast between the restrained white exterior and the warm, colorful interior is the highlight. Inside, the prayer hall is decorated with blue-and-gold painted patterns, floral and geometric designs, and Quranic calligraphy around the dome, along with carved wooden ornaments on the balconies and railings in the Georgian woodworking tradition. The minaret and gilded dome are landmarks of the older Batumi skyline.

batumiDolphinarium.heroTitle: Batumi Dolphinarium: Shows and Visitor Information
batumiDolphinarium.content:
The Batumi Dolphinarium is one of the most visited family attractions in Batumi, on the Black Sea coast of Adjara in Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus. Located in a park near the city center, it has operated dolphin shows since 1975, when it opened as the first dolphinarium in the former Soviet Union, and it remains a long-established fixture of the city's tourism. It draws large audiences in the summer months when Batumi is at its busiest, and for families traveling with children it's one of the more straightforward indoor-style activity options the city offers.

What is the Batumi Dolphinarium?

The Batumi Dolphinarium is a marine-mammal venue near central Batumi (at 51 Rustaveli Avenue, by 6 May Park) that has run dolphin shows since 1975. Performances feature bottlenose dolphins and California sea lions and last around 30–45 minutes, with two or three shows on a typical day and an adjacent aquarium of around 100 species. Show tickets are inexpensive (roughly 20–25 GEL); a separate, higher-priced swim-with-dolphins option is also offered. It's a popular family attraction, busiest in July and August, and advance booking is advisable in peak season.

The shows

Performances run for roughly thirty to forty-five minutes and follow the established format of marine-mammal shows — trained dolphins and sea lions performing routines of jumps, synchronized movements, ball-and-hoop acrobatics, and interactions with trainers, with some segments bringing the animals close to the audience (front rows can get splashed). Commentary is in Georgian and English, and the show is built to be accessible regardless of the viewer's age or language. There's an aquarium beside the dolphinarium displaying around 100 species, mostly Black Sea and Georgian freshwater fish, which can be combined with the show.

Two to three shows typically run on a given day during the season, with the schedule reduced in the shoulder and winter months; published times have included midday and afternoon performances with an added evening show in peak season. The specific timetable changes seasonally, so it's worth checking current show times before planning a visit, particularly outside the peak July–August period.

Swimming with dolphins

The dolphinarium also offers a swim-with-dolphins option, separate from the standard show and priced considerably higher (sources have cited around 150–200 GEL, lasting about fifteen minutes), with limited places per session and advance booking required. Travelers considering this should be aware that experiences involving captive marine mammals — and swim-with-dolphin programs in particular — are a subject of ongoing animal-welfare debate, and a number of international travel companies have moved away from selling them. It's a personal decision worth making with that context in mind.

Practical considerations

The dolphinarium is most crowded in July and August, when shows can fill to capacity, so arriving early and booking tickets in advance is advisable in that period. Buying from the official channels (the venue or its official website) rather than informal street sellers is wise, as resellers have been reported charging inflated prices. Children under a certain age are generally admitted free if they don't need a separate seat.

The surrounding park is pleasant for a short walk before or after a visit, and the central location near the Batumi boulevard means it can be combined naturally with time on the seafront or other nearby attractions.

Practical information

  • Location: 51 Rustaveli Avenue, Batumi, by 6 May Park (central, walkable from the boulevard; reachable by bus, minibus, or taxi).
  • Founded: 1975 (the first dolphinarium in the former Soviet Union).
  • Animals: Bottlenose dolphins and California sea lions; adjacent aquarium (~100 species).
  • Shows: ~30–45 minutes, in Georgian and English; two to three daily in season (schedule reduced off-season).
  • Tickets: Show tickets roughly 20–25 GEL; swim-with-dolphins priced separately and much higher. Available at the venue and online via the official site.
  • Best to know: Front rows may get splashed; busiest in July–August; buy from official channels only.

faq.title: What is the Batumi Dolphinarium?
faq.content: It's a marine-mammal venue near central Batumi, at 51 Rustaveli Avenue by 6 May Park, that has run dolphin shows since 1975 — it was the first dolphinarium in the former Soviet Union. Shows feature bottlenose dolphins and California sea lions and last about 30–45 minutes, and there's an adjacent aquarium. It's one of Batumi's most popular family attractions, especially in summer.

faq.title: Where is the Batumi Dolphinarium and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's at 51 Rustaveli Avenue, next to 6 May Park, a fairly central location reachable on foot from the boulevard area or by bus, minibus, or taxi. Parking is available nearby. It's easy to combine with time on the Batumi seafront or other central attractions.

faq.title: What are the show times and ticket prices?
faq.content: Two to three shows typically run on a given day in the summer season, with a reduced schedule in the shoulder and winter months; times have included midday, afternoon, and an added evening show in peak season. Show tickets are inexpensive (around 20–25 GEL). Because the schedule and prices change seasonally, it's best to check the venue's official website before visiting, and to buy from official channels rather than street resellers.

faq.title: Can you swim with the dolphins at the Batumi Dolphinarium?
faq.content: Yes, a swim-with-dolphins option is offered separately from the standard show, with limited places and advance booking required, at a considerably higher price (cited around 150–200 GEL for roughly fifteen minutes). Note that programs involving captive marine mammals are a subject of ongoing animal-welfare debate, and many international travel companies no longer sell them — worth considering before booking.

faq.title: Is the Batumi Dolphinarium good for children?
faq.content: It's one of Batumi's most popular family attractions, and the shows are designed to be accessible to all ages, with commentary in Georgian and English. Younger children under a certain age are usually admitted free if they don't need a separate seat. Note that front-row seats can get splashed during the show, which children often enjoy but is worth knowing in advance.

batumiPiazza.heroTitle: Batumi Piazza: The Italianate Square in the City Center
batumiPiazza.content:
Batumi Piazza is a purpose-built pedestrian square in the center of Batumi, in Adjara on the Black Sea coast of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus. Designed in an Italianate style and built around 2009–2010 as part of the broader transformation of Batumi's urban fabric during the modernization program of that era, it sits a short walk from Europe Square and the historic center. Its colorful facades, arcaded ground floors, mosaic paving, and central clock tower create an environment deliberately evocative of a Venetian or Italian piazza — a reference that is openly artificial, but executed with enough care and detail to produce a space that works as a place to spend time rather than just a backdrop for photographs.

What is Batumi Piazza?

Batumi Piazza is an Italian-style pedestrian square in central Batumi, built around 2009–2010 and designed by Georgian architect Vazha Orbeladze with inspiration from Venice. Covering about 5,700 square meters, it's enclosed by ochre-and-terracotta buildings with arcades, cafés, restaurants, and small hotels, and its highlights are a St. Mark's–style clock tower, decorative stained-glass windows, and a large central marble mosaic — said to be the largest figurative marble mosaic in Europe. It's a lively social and dining space, especially in the evening, and a natural stop on a walk through the city center. It's a public square, free and open at all times.

The square and its art

The square is enclosed on its principal sides by four-to-five-story buildings whose facades are painted in warm ochres, terracottas, and creams, with decorative details, balconies, and arcades at ground level housing the cafés, restaurants, and small hotels that give the space its social life. Designed by the Georgian architect Vazha Orbeladze — known for restoration work on historic buildings in Tbilisi, Sighnaghi, and Telavi — the complex draws on Venetian architecture, and the clock tower at one end, styled after the campanile of St. Mark's, provides the vertical focal point that pulls the composition together the way a campanile anchors an Italian square.

Two pieces of decorative art lift the square above a simple architectural pastiche. The central marble mosaic — created in 2010 to designs by the Georgian-Swiss artist Natalia Amirejibi de Pita, and described as the largest figurative marble mosaic in Europe (around 106 square meters) — has a marine theme, with concentric circles like whirlpools and five female sea-deity figures at its center. (It was assembled from millions of small marble pieces, fabricated at a workshop in Abu Dhabi and laid in Batumi under the artist's supervision.) The buildings also carry decorative stained-glass windows by the Estonian artist Dolores Hoffman. The mosaic in particular rewards close attention — its patterns are more intricate than they appear from a distance, and it's best appreciated from one of the upper vantage points around the square.

The experience

Piazza's primary appeal is experiential rather than cultural or historical. This isn't a place with layers of significance to decode or a monument with a story to understand — it's a well-made social space designed to be pleasant to sit in, and it succeeds at that more reliably than many more ambitious urban interventions. The cafés and restaurants in the arcaded ground floors extend their seating into the square in the warmer months, and the combination of the enclosed spatial character, the decorative architecture above, and the outdoor tables below creates a setting for an evening meal or a drink that has few equivalents in Batumi.

Live music — often a pianist or a small ensemble — appears in the evenings with some regularity, and the acoustics of the enclosed square carry it without overwhelming conversation at the surrounding tables; the square has also hosted larger cultural events and concerts over the years. The evening atmosphere, when the warm facade lighting comes on and the space fills with a mix of locals and visitors, is one of the more reliably pleasant experiences in the city center, regardless of season. The daytime experience is quieter, and the architectural and mosaic details are easier to examine without the evening crowds.

Location and connections

Piazza sits within easy walking distance of Europe Square and the historic center, the Batumi boulevard and seafront, and the Old Town streets, making it a natural stop on any walk through the central districts. Combining an evening at Piazza with a stroll along the boulevard and a stop at the nearby Ali & Nino statue or the Alphabetic Tower takes in both the modern seafront architecture and the more intimate scale of the square in a single continuous circuit, without requiring any particular planning. It's tucked behind the surrounding buildings and easy to walk past without noticing, so it's worth looking for the entrances from the surrounding streets; the cobblestone streets around it are largely closed to traffic, so the approach is on foot.

Practical information

  • Location: Piazza (Piazza Square), central Batumi, in the Old Town near Europe Square; reached on foot from the surrounding streets (the immediate streets are pedestrianized).
  • Hours: The square itself is open at all times; cafés and restaurants typically run from mid-morning until midnight or later, varying by establishment.
  • Admission: Free (the square is a public space; cafés, restaurants, and hotels charge separately).
  • Best time: Evening for the atmosphere, live music, and lit facades; daytime for examining the mosaic and architecture in quiet.

faq.title: What is Batumi Piazza?
faq.content: Batumi Piazza is an Italian-style pedestrian square in the center of Batumi, built around 2009–2010 and designed by Georgian architect Vazha Orbeladze with inspiration from Venice. It covers about 5,700 square meters, enclosed by warm-colored arcaded buildings housing cafés, restaurants, and small hotels, with a St. Mark's–style clock tower, stained-glass windows, and a large central marble mosaic. It's one of the city's most popular social and dining spots.

faq.title: Is Batumi Piazza historic or modern?
faq.content: It's modern — a purpose-built square completed around 2010, deliberately designed to evoke a classic Italian piazza rather than a genuinely old one. Its appeal is the atmosphere and craft rather than history: the architecture, the Venetian-style clock tower, the stained glass, and especially the marble mosaic at its center are all recent works, made to create a convincing and pleasant European-style square in the heart of Batumi.

faq.title: What is the mosaic at Batumi Piazza?
faq.content: The square's centerpiece is a large marble mosaic created in 2010 by Georgian-Swiss artist Natalia Amirejibi de Pita, described as the largest figurative marble mosaic in Europe (around 106 square meters). It has a marine theme — concentric circles like whirlpools, with five female sea-deity figures at the center — and was fabricated from millions of small marble pieces at a workshop in Abu Dhabi and assembled in Batumi. It's best viewed from an elevated vantage point.

faq.title: When is the best time to visit Batumi Piazza?
faq.content: Evening is the highlight — the facades are lit, the cafés and restaurants set out their tables, and there's often live music (a pianist or small ensemble), giving the square a warm, lively atmosphere year-round. Daytime is quieter and better for examining the mosaic, stained glass, and architecture without the crowds. The square is open at all times, and it's free to enter.

faq.title: Where is Batumi Piazza and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in the heart of Old Batumi, a 5–10 minute walk from the historic center, and within easy reach of Europe Square, the Old Town, and the boulevard. The immediate streets are pedestrianized cobblestone, so you reach it on foot; it's tucked behind the surrounding buildings, so look for the entrances from the nearby streets. There's no dedicated parking, so walking (or a taxi to the edge of the area) is easiest.

batumiDancingFountains.heroTitle: Dancing Fountains of Batumi: The Ardagani Lake Water and Light Show
batumiDancingFountains.content:
The Dancing Fountains occupy the Ardagani Lake section of the Batumi Boulevard, in Adjara on the Black Sea coast of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — where the promenade widens around a decorative lake that becomes the stage for a synchronized water and light show in the evenings. It's one of the most popular gathering points along the seafront after dark, drawing a steady crowd of locals and visitors who settle on the surrounding benches and café terraces to watch the performance play out over the water.

What are the Batumi Dancing Fountains?

The Batumi Dancing Fountains are an evening water, light, and music show on Ardagani Lake, in the New Boulevard section of the Batumi seafront. Installed around 2009, the fountains' jets are choreographed to music — from Georgian songs to international pop, rock, and classical pieces — with colored lights and an accompanying laser show projected onto the spray. The show runs free, after dark (commonly cited from around 9 pm through late evening), in cycles rather than a single fixed performance. It's a relaxed, family-friendly highlight of Batumi's nightlife. (Batumi also has smaller dancing-fountain displays elsewhere, but the Ardagani Lake one is the largest.)

The show

The show is what the name suggests — water jets choreographed to music, their height and direction shifting in rhythm with the soundtrack while colored lights move across the spray and the lake surface below. The programming cycles through a range of music, typically mixing international popular songs (compositions by world-famous artists feature regularly) with classical and operatic pieces, and the different musical moods produce different visual effects as the patterns respond to the tempo and character of each track. A laser show accompanies the water display, casting patterns and imagery across the mist thrown up by the jets — some of it themed around the history of Batumi and Georgia — adding a further dimension to the performance.

Worth knowing: Ardagani Lake is the largest and most elaborate of Batumi's dancing fountains, but it isn't the only one — there are additional displays elsewhere along the seafront and in the city center (including on the Old Boulevard and near Europe Square), so you may come across more than one on a walk through town.

The experience

The fountains are best understood as an ambient evening attraction rather than a structured performance with a fixed start and end. They run in cycles through the evening with brief pauses between sequences, so arriving at any point after dark gives access to the full experience without needing to time a visit to a specific showtime (shows are commonly cited as running from around 9 pm into the early hours in high season). Most people watch from a relaxed position — a bench around the lake, a table at a surrounding café, a spot on the promenade — rather than a fixed viewing point, and the social atmosphere of the area is as much a part of the experience as the fountains themselves.

The reflections of the colored lights on the lake surface double the visual effect, and the combination of moving water, shifting color, reflected light, and warm evening air creates the kind of effortless pleasure that makes this one of the more consistently attended spots on the boulevard. Families with children find it particularly engaging — the scale is accessible rather than overwhelming, and the mix of light, color, and movement holds younger attention well. (The jets are powerful, so sitting very close to the lake edge can mean getting lightly sprayed.)

The Ardagani Lake area is one of the livelier sections of the boulevard in the evening, with restaurants and cafés clustered around the waterfront and the steady pedestrian flow of the promenade bringing continuous activity. Combining the fountain show with a walk along the full length of the boulevard — ending or beginning at the lake — covers the most animated stretch of Batumi's evening outdoor life in a single continuous route.

Practical information

  • Location: Ardagani Lake, in the New Boulevard section of the Batumi seafront, Batumi, Adjara.
  • Hours: After dark through the evening; commonly cited as running from around 9 pm into the early hours in summer, in cycles with short pauses. Reduced or paused outside the peak season.
  • Admission: Free.
  • Best time: A clear summer evening, when the full program (and the laser show) is most likely to run.
  • Combine with: A walk along the Batumi boulevard and the nearby seafront landmarks (the Ali & Nino statue, the Alphabetic Tower).

faq.title: What are the Batumi Dancing Fountains?
faq.content: They're an evening water, light, and music show on Ardagani Lake, in the New Boulevard section of the Batumi seafront. Water jets are choreographed to music — Georgian songs alongside international pop, rock, and classical pieces — with colored lights and a laser show projected onto the spray. Installed around 2009, it's a free, relaxed, family-friendly attraction and one of the most popular after-dark gathering spots on the boulevard.

faq.title: What time do the Batumi Dancing Fountains run?
faq.content: The show runs after dark through the evening, commonly cited as starting around 9 pm and continuing into the early hours during the summer season, in cycles with short pauses rather than as a single fixed performance — so arriving any time after dark gives you the full experience. The schedule is reduced or paused outside the peak months, and the laser show in particular doesn't always run on a fixed timetable, so it's worth checking locally.

faq.title: Where are the dancing fountains in Batumi?
faq.content: The main and largest display is at Ardagani Lake, in the New Boulevard part of the seafront. Batumi actually has more than one set of dancing fountains — there are additional displays elsewhere along the seafront and in the city center, including on the Old Boulevard and near Europe Square — but the Ardagani Lake fountains, with their laser show, are the most elaborate and the ones most visitors mean by "the Batumi dancing fountains."

faq.title: Are the Batumi Dancing Fountains free, and are they good for kids?
faq.content: Yes, the show is completely free, viewed from the benches, café terraces, and promenade around the lake. It's particularly good for children — the scale is accessible and the mix of light, color, music, and moving water holds their attention well. The area is lively in the evening with cafés and restaurants nearby. Note the jets are powerful, so sitting right at the lake edge can mean getting lightly sprayed.

faq.title: When is the best time to see the dancing fountains?
faq.content: A clear summer evening is ideal, when the full program (including the laser show) is most likely to run and the surrounding boulevard is at its liveliest. The fountains operate daily through the peak season (roughly June–September) with a reduced or paused schedule outside it. Pairing the show with an evening stroll along the boulevard, taking in the lit seafront landmarks, makes for one of Batumi's best free evenings out.

europeSquareBatumi.heroTitle: Europe Square, Batumi: The Medea Statue and the Golden Fleece
europeSquareBatumi.content:
Europe Square sits at the heart of central Batumi, in Adjara on the Black Sea coast of Georgia — the country in the South Caucasus — a well-proportioned open space surrounded by buildings in a European architectural style that gives this part of the city its most consistently elegant character. It's one of the main orientation points in the city center and a natural gathering place for residents and visitors alike, its cafés and terraces reliably occupied through the day and into the evening in the warmer months.

What is Europe Square?

Europe Square is the elegant central plaza of Batumi, redeveloped during the city's 2000s–2010s transformation, surrounded by European-style facades, cafés, and restaurants. At its center stands a tall column topped by the gilded statue of Medea holding the Golden Fleece — a reference to the ancient kingdom of Colchis (western Georgia), the destination of Jason and the Argonauts in Greek myth. The square is a free, lively public space, especially in the evening, and a natural starting point for exploring central Batumi: the Piazza, the boulevard, and the Old Town are all a short walk away.

The square

Europe Square was developed and substantially redesigned during the urban transformation of Batumi in the 2000s and 2010s, when the city invested heavily in its public spaces as part of a broader effort to position itself as a regional tourism destination. The surrounding buildings — decorative 19th- and early-20th-century facades from Batumi's oil-boom era, alongside newer additions, with balconies and arcade-level ground floors housing restaurants and shops — were arranged to read as a coherent European city square, and the result feels noticeably more ordered and architecturally unified than much of the rest of the city. The cobblestone paving and the general quality of the public realm give Europe Square a refinement that sets it apart from Batumi's more improvised districts. One building on the square carries a notable astronomical clock (made in Germany and installed around 2010) that displays not only the time but astronomical information such as the moon phase.

The Medea statue

At the center of the square stands a tall column topped by a gilded figure of Medea holding the Golden Fleece aloft. The statue was created by the Georgian sculptor Davit Khmaladze and unveiled in 2007, and the ship Argo is depicted on its pedestal. The choice of Medea for Batumi's principal square is a deliberate invocation of the ancient geography that placed the land of Colchis — the destination of Jason and the Argonauts in their quest for the Golden Fleece — on the eastern shore of the Black Sea, in what is now western Georgia.

In the myth, Medea was the daughter of Aeetes, king of Colchis; she fell in love with Jason, helped him obtain the Golden Fleece, and fled with him aboard the Argo. One of the most famous stories in the Greek tradition, it is reclaimed here with Medea as a Colchian — that is, a Georgian — figure rather than simply a character in a Greek story. The Fleece she holds (actually gilded) refers to the legend that gave the Colchian civilization its enduring association with wealth and gold in the ancient imagination — a legend often linked to the real Colchian practice of using sheep's fleeces to catch gold particles in mountain streams. Placing this mythological identity at the literal center of a square named for Europe connects the deep past to the contemporary, westward-looking aspiration the city has invested in the square's name.

The statue is visible from the approaches to the square in most directions, and its gilded surface catches the light in a way that gives the center of the space a focal point the surrounding architecture, pleasant as it is, cannot match for visual impact.

The square in use

Europe Square functions differently through the day in ways that make the timing of a visit worth considering. In the morning it's calm, the cafés filling gradually and the cobblestones relatively clear of the later crowds. By midday the surrounding restaurants are busy with lunch, and the afternoon brings a steady flow of pedestrian activity that peaks in the early evening, when the lighting of the surrounding buildings creates the most atmospheric version of the space.

The evening transformation of Europe Square is one of the better examples in Batumi of how the city's public spaces come alive after dark — the warm facade lighting, the full café and restaurant terraces, and the general social energy combine to produce an environment that genuinely rewards an extended stay rather than a brief photo stop. Street musicians perform here on occasion, and in the summer season the square is one of the more reliably animated spots in the city center. It's also the city's main venue for festive events — concerts, festivals, and New Year celebrations are held here.

Connections

Europe Square connects naturally to the adjacent areas of central Batumi. The Piazza is a short walk to the east, a more enclosed and intimate complement to Europe Square's openness. The Batumi boulevard and the seafront are a few minutes' walk to the west. The Old Town streets — where Batumi's late-19th- and early-20th-century oil-boom architecture survives most intact — begin immediately to the north and east. The concentration of these different urban environments within a compact, walkable area makes Europe Square a natural starting point for exploring the central districts on foot, and a convenient anchor for any city-center walking route.

Practical information

  • Location: Europe Square, central Batumi, Adjara (on Memed Abashidze Avenue, in the Old Town area).
  • Hours: Open at all times; surrounding cafés and restaurants typically run from morning until midnight or later.
  • Admission: Free (public square; cafés and restaurants charge separately).
  • Best time: Early evening for the atmosphere and lit facades; morning for a quieter look at the architecture and the Medea statue.
  • Combine with: The Piazza, the Batumi boulevard, and an Old Town walk.

faq.title: What is Europe Square in Batumi?
faq.content: Europe Square is the elegant central plaza of Batumi, redeveloped during the city's 2000s–2010s transformation and surrounded by European-style facades, cafés, and restaurants. At its center is a tall column topped by the gilded statue of Medea holding the Golden Fleece. It's a free, lively public space — busiest and most atmospheric in the evening — and one of the main orientation points and gathering places in the city center.

faq.title: Who is the statue in Europe Square?
faq.content: It's a statue of Medea, the princess of ancient Colchis from Greek mythology, created by Georgian sculptor Davit Khmaladze and unveiled in 2007. She holds the Golden Fleece aloft, and the ship Argo is depicted on the pedestal. The statue ties Batumi to the legend of Jason and the Argonauts — Colchis, their destination, lay in what is now western Georgia — reclaiming Medea as a Georgian figure and symbol of the region's ancient wealth.

faq.title: Why is the Golden Fleece connected to Batumi and Georgia?
faq.content: In Greek myth, Jason and the Argonauts sailed to Colchis — on the eastern Black Sea, in present-day western Georgia — to win the Golden Fleece, and Medea, the Colchian king's daughter, helped them. The legend is often linked to a real Colchian practice of using sheep's fleeces to trap gold particles in mountain streams, which may explain the region's ancient association with gold. The Medea statue makes that heritage the centerpiece of the square.

faq.title: What is there to do at Europe Square?
faq.content: It's mainly a place to sit, eat, and soak up the atmosphere — the surrounding cafés and restaurants set out terraces, and the square is liveliest in the evening when the facades are lit. It's also Batumi's main venue for festive events like concerts and New Year celebrations. Beyond that, it's the best starting point for a city-center walk, with the Piazza, the Old Town, and the boulevard all a few minutes away.

faq.title: Where is Europe Square and how do you get there?
faq.content: It's in the heart of central Batumi, on Memed Abashidze Avenue in the Old Town area, a few minutes' walk from the Batumi boulevard and the seafront, with the Piazza just to the east. It's a compact, walkable part of the city, so most visitors reach it on foot; taxis and buses also serve the center. It's free and open at all times.
